Examiners deduct **1 mark** for poor memory hygiene. > 4. **Not including `SJMP HALT` at the end.** Without the idle loop, the 8051 PC rolls over past `FFFFH` to `0000H` and re-executes the program, which the examiner interprets as a logic error. **(-0.5 to 1 mark)** > 5. **Forgetting to specify `ORG` directives.** Without `ORG 0000H` and `ORG 0030H`, the assembler places the code at the default origin, often clashing with the interrupt-vector table at `0003H`, `000BH`, etc. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024** > - **Algorithm:** Initialize → Fetch → Add → Carry-Propagate → Update-Pointer → Test-Counter. > - **Key Instructions:** `MOV A,@R0` (fetch), `ADD A,R1` (add), `JNC SKIP` (carry test), `INC R2` (carry store), `INC R0` (pointer advance), `DJNZ R7,LOOP` (loop control). > - **Register Allocation:** `R0` = pointer, `R1` = Sum_L, `R2` = Sum_H, `R7` = counter. > - **Memory Convention:** Array starts at `30H`; results stored at `60H` and `61H`. > - **16-bit result rule:** When total sum exceeds `FF₁₆`, a register pair (typically `R1:R2`) is mandatory. > - **Carry-Count Identity:** $S_{\text{high}} = $ total number of carry-out events. > - **External Memory:** Use `MOVX` and `DPTR`; internal `MOV` cannot access it. > - **Pointer Width:** 8-bit (`R0`/`R1`) for internal, 16-bit (`DPTR`) for external. > - **Loop Primitive:** `DJNZ` is preferred over `DEC + JNZ` (saves 1 byte and 1 machine cycle). > - **Execution Time:** $T_{\text{exec}} = N \times 5 \times T_{\text{mc}}$ for the canonical 3-instruction inner loop. > - **Termination:** Always end with `SJMP HALT` to prevent program-counter rollover. > - **Assembly Directive:** `ORG` specifies code origin; `DB` defines byte constants; `EQU` defines symbolic constants. > - **Standard Crystal:** **11.0592 MHz** → $T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085\,\mu s$.

topics.title: Sorting - Ascending and Descending Order
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sorting - Ascending and Descending Order

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PCBRL408 - Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems Lab**, **Sorting** is formally defined as the systematic rearrangement of a finite sequence of data elements stored in the internal **RAM (Random Access Memory)** of a target microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051** family) into either **Ascending Order** (lowest value at the lowest memory address, highest value at the highest memory address) or **Descending Order** (highest value at the lowest memory address, lowest value at the highest memory address).

The sorting operation is executed entirely within the **internal data memory** (IRAM, 128 bytes, addresses `0x00` to `0x7F`) of the **8051 microcontroller** using either:
- **Assembly Language Programming (ALP)** executed on the **Keil µVision IDE** and simulated using the built-in **8051 Simulator** (a mandatory tool in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus).
- Embedded C programming cross-compiled via the **Keil C51** toolchain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Practical Mandate:** Students are required to write the sorting algorithm in **8051 Assembly Language**, simulate it on **Keil µVision 5**, verify the output in the **Memory Window** and **Watch Window**, and record the results in the **Observation Notebook** as part of the **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — The Two Memory Models:**
> - **Internal RAM (IRAM):** 128 bytes of general-purpose RAM (locations `0x00`–`0x7F`). Used for storing the **unsorted array**, the **sorted array**, and the **loop counters**.
> - **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** Memory-mapped registers at addresses `0x80`–`0xFF`. Accumulators (`A` / `0xE0`), `B` register (`0xF0`), `R0`–`R7` banks, and the **Program Counter (PC)** reside here.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **six unsorted cricket balls** lying on a table, each with a different weight printed on it: **52, 17, 88, 31, 64, 9** grams.

- **Ascending Sorting** is like arranging these balls from the **lightest ball (9 g)** on the left to the **heaviest ball (88 g)** on the right. The microcontroller does the same: it scans through memory locations and arranges bytes so the **smallest numerical value sits at the lowest RAM address** and the **largest value sits at the highest RAM address**.
- **Descending Sorting** is the exact opposite — the **heaviest ball (88 g)** goes to the left, and the **lightest ball (9 g)** goes to the right. In RAM terms, the **largest value is placed at the lowest address** and the **smallest value at the highest address**.

The microcontroller, being a deterministic sequential machine, performs this rearrangement by **repeatedly comparing adjacent memory contents**, swapping them whenever they are out of order, and iterating this process until the entire array is sorted. This is the essence of the **Bubble Sort** algorithm — the most common sorting routine taught in the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Module 2**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Address Analogy:** Think of RAM as a row of **mailboxes numbered 30H, 31H, 32H, …, 35H**. The 8051 looks into each mailbox, compares the "letter" (byte) inside with its neighbor's letter, and swaps letters until they are in the desired order — just like a postal worker organizing mail.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Boundary Constants

| Term | Definition | Typical Value (8051 Context) |
|---|---|---|
| **Array Size (N)** | Number of elements to be sorted | **05H to 0AH** (5–10 elements, typical lab size) |
| **Starting Address** | Base RAM location of the array | **30H** (commonly used safe internal RAM) |
| **End Address** | Last RAM location of the array | **30H + N − 1** |
| **Loop Counter 1 (Outer)** | Tracks number of passes | **R0** or **R7** |
| **Loop Counter 2 (Inner)** | Tracks comparisons within a pass | **R1** or **R6** |
| **Swap Register** | Temporary storage for byte exchange | **Accumulator `A` (0xE0)** |
| **Swap Register 2** | Secondary temporary storage | **`B` register (0xF0)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Highlight:** The **8051 uses the Accumulator (`A`) as the primary swap register** because it is the only register capable of direct arithmetic and data transfer with internal RAM via the **`MOV`**, **`XCH`**, and **`CJNE`** instructions. The **standard size of an 8051 instruction is 1 to 3 bytes**, and the **clock period is 12 oscillator cycles per machine cycle** (i.e., at 11.0592 MHz crystal, one machine cycle = **1.085 µs**).

---

### 1.4 Why Sorting Matters in Embedded Systems

Sorting is not merely an academic exercise in embedded systems — it is a **fundamental data processing primitive** with real-world engineering applications:

- **Sensor Data Ranking:** A temperature monitoring system with **8 distributed sensors** must rank the readings to identify the hottest and coldest zones for HVAC control.
- **Median Filtering:** Removing impulse noise from ADC samples requires identifying the **median value** — which itself requires sorted data.
- **Priority Scheduling:** Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) like **FreeRTOS** sort task priorities to determine execution order.
- **Display Systems:** Seven-segment and LCD-based industrial displays often show values in **ascending order** for clear user interpretation.
- **Communication Protocols:** The **I²C** address arbitration and **CAN bus** message filtering rely on sorted identifier lists.

---

### 1.5 Learning Outcomes Alignment (KTU CO Mapping)

Upon completion of this experiment, the student will be able to:

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | Write and execute 8051 ALP programs for data manipulation operations including sorting | **Apply (Level 3)** |
| **CO2** | Analyze sorting algorithms by tracing instruction-level execution on the Keil simulator | **Analyze (Level 4)** |
| **CO3** | Debug and verify assembly programs using the **Memory Window**, **Watch Window**, and **Register Window** of Keil µVision | **Evaluate (Level 5)** |
| **CO4** | Document experimental results in the prescribed **KTU Lab Record** format | **Create (Level 6)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bubble Sort Algorithm — Operational Theory

The **Bubble Sort** is the **canonical sorting algorithm** prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme PCBRL408 syllabus for Module Experiment 2. Its name derives from the way smaller (or larger) elements "bubble up" toward their correct positions during each pass, analogous to air bubbles rising in water.

#### 2.1.1 Algorithm Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The Bubble Sort algorithm operates on an array of **N** data elements stored in consecutive internal RAM locations. The sorting proceeds in **two nested loops**:

**Outer Loop (Pass Counter):**
- The outer loop runs for **N − 1 passes** (where N is the array size).
- Each pass guarantees that at least one element reaches its final sorted position.
- In the **8051**, this is typically managed by register **R7** (or **R0**) acting as the pass counter.

**Inner Loop (Comparison Counter):**
- Within each pass, the inner loop performs **N − 1 − i** comparisons, where **i** is the current pass index (0-based).
- Each comparison examines two adjacent array elements and swaps them if they violate the desired order.
- In the **8051**, this is typically managed by register **R6** (or **R1**) acting as the comparison counter.

**Swap Mechanism:**
- For ascending order: if `array[j] > array[j+1]`, swap them.
- For descending order: if `array[j] < array[j+1]`, swap them.
- The **8051's `XCH` (Exchange)** instruction atomically swaps the contents of the Accumulator with a specified RAM location — making it the perfect single-instruction swap primitive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Note (Beyond Syllabus but Examiner-Appreciated):** A **swap flag** (boolean variable) can be introduced to detect passes with no swaps, indicating an already-sorted array and allowing early termination. While not strictly required by KTU, mentioning this demonstrates deeper understanding and can fetch partial extra marks.

---

### 2.2 The 8051 Instruction Set Primitives for Sorting

The following instruction set forms the **vocabulary** with which sorting algorithms are constructed in 8051 ALP:

| Mnemonic | Full Form | Operation | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **`MOV A, @R0`** | Move to A from RAM pointed by R0 | `A ← (R0)` | 1 | 1 |
| **`MOV @R0, A`** | Move to RAM from A | `(R0) ← A` | 1 | 1 |
| **`MOV DPTR, #data`** | Load Data Pointer | `DPTR ← data16` | 3 | 2 |
| **`INC R0`** | Increment R0 | `R0 ← R0 + 1` | 1 | 1 |
| **`DEC R1`** | Decrement R1 | `R1 ← R1 − 1` | 1 | 1 |
| **`DJNZ R7, label`** | Decrement and Jump if Not Zero | `R7 ← R7 − 1; if R7 ≠ 0, jump` | 2 | 2 |
| **`CJNE A, direct, rel`** | Compare and Jump if Not Equal | `if A ≠ (direct), jump to rel` | 3 | 2 |
| **`XCH A, @R0`** | Exchange A with RAM | `A ↔ (R0)` | 1 | 1 |
| **`SWAP A`** | Swap nibbles of A | `A[7:4] ↔ A[3:0]` | 1 | 1 |
| **`CLR C`** | Clear Carry flag | `C ← 0` | 1 | 1 |
| **`JC label`** | Jump if Carry set | `if C = 1, jump to label` | 2 | 2 |
| **`SJMP label`** | Short Jump | `PC ← PC + offset` | 2 | 2 |
| **`NOP`** | No Operation | `PC ← PC + 1` | 1 | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watchpoint:** Students frequently confuse **`MOV A, R0`** (which moves the **contents** of R0 to A) with **`MOV A, @R0`** (which moves the **contents of the RAM location pointed by** R0 to A). This single-character distinction (`@`) is the most common source of bugs in sorting programs. **Always remember: `@` means "indirect" / "the thing pointed to."**

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Ascending & Descending Sorting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Table Cells:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is replaced with `\vert` inside mathematical expressions to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Parameter / Concept | Ascending Order Formula or Rule | Descending Order Formula or Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Sort Direction** | Smallest → Largest | Largest → Smallest |
| **Memory Placement** | Low address = Smallest value | Low address = Largest value |
| **Swap Condition** | `array[j] > array[j+1]` | `array[j] < array[j+1]` |
| **Final State (Lowest Addr)** | `array[0] = min(A)` | `array[0] = max(A)` |
| **Final State (Highest Addr)** | `array[N−1] = max(A)` | `array[N−1] = min(A)` |
| **Comparison Instruction** | `CJNE A, 30h, label` (uses unsigned `vert` jump) | `CJNE A, 30h, label` (same mnemonic, logic differs) |
| **Comparator (8051 Logic)** | `if (A) > (RAM), swap` | `if (A) < (RAM), swap` |
| **Number of Passes** | $P_{\text{asc}} = N - 1$ | $P_{\text{desc}} = N - 1$ |
| **Comparisons per Pass $i$** | $C_{i,\text{asc}} = N - 1 - i$ | $C_{i,\text{desc}} = N - 1 - i$ |
| **Total Comparisons (Worst Case)** | $T_{\text{asc}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $T_{\text{desc}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ |
| **Total Swaps (Worst Case)** | $S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $S_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(N^{2})$ | $O(N^{2})$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ — in-place sort | $O(1)$ — in-place sort |
| **Algorithm Stability** | Stable (equal keys preserve order) | Stable (equal keys preserve order) |
| **8051 Array Size (Lab)** | Typically $N = 05_{\text{H}}$ | Typically $N = 05_{\text{H}}$ |
| **Common Start Address** | `30H` | `30H` |
| **End Address Calculation** | $\text{EndAddr}_{\text{asc}} = \text{StartAddr} + N - 1$ | $\text{EndAddr}_{\text{desc}} = \text{StartAddr} + N - 1$ |

---

### 2.4 Mathematical Foundations of Bubble Sort Complexity

The **Total Number of Comparisons** in the worst case is derived from the arithmetic series of the inner loop:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-2} (N - 1 - i)
$$

Expanding the summation:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (N-1) + (N-2) + (N-3) + \cdots + 2 + 1
$$

Applying the formula for the sum of the first $M$ natural numbers (where $M = N - 1$):

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{M(M+1)}{2} = \dfrac{(N-1) \cdot N}{2}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

The **Asymptotic Time Complexity** is obtained by extracting the highest-order term:

$$
T(N) = O(N^{2}) = \Theta\!\left(\dfrac{N^{2} - N}{2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For $N = 10$ (a typical lab-sized array), the total comparisons required = $\frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$. At 11.0592 MHz crystal (1.085 µs per machine cycle, 2 cycles per `CJNE`), each comparison takes approximately **2.17 µs**, making the entire worst-case sort duration approximately **97.65 µs** — blazingly fast for human perception but measurable on an oscilloscope.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bubble Sort, despite its $O(N^{2})$ complexity, finds niche applications in embedded systems because:

- **Predictable Execution Time:** The worst-case execution time is deterministic and bounded — critical for **hard real-time systems** where Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) analysis is mandatory.
- **Minimal Code Footprint:** The 8051 ALP implementation requires only **~20–30 bytes of program memory** (code space) and **zero additional RAM** beyond the array itself.
- **Cache-Friendly Access Pattern:** Sequential memory access perfectly matches the **8051's Harvard architecture** where program and data memories are separate.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Project Hint:** In final-year projects involving **sensor fusion** or **threshold-based alarm systems**, sorting is often used to compute the **k-th order statistic** (e.g., the 3rd largest of 5 sensor readings) for **majority voting** or **N-version programming** fault-tolerance schemes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ascending Sort (Exhaustive Trace)

**Problem Statement:** Sort the following array of **5 unsigned bytes** stored in the 8051 internal RAM starting at address `30H` into **ascending order** using the **Bubble Sort algorithm**.

**Initial Array State:**

| RAM Address | `30H` | `31H` | `32H` | `33H` | `34H` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value (Hex)** | `52H` | `17H` | `88H` | `31H` | `09H` |
| **Value (Dec)** | 82 | 23 | 136 | 49 | 9 |

**Target:** After sorting, the array should read `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` in ascending order.

---

### 3.2 Assembly Language Program (Ascending Order)

Below is the **complete, fully-commented, KTU-standard 8051 ALP program** for ascending sorting of an array of 5 bytes stored at internal RAM location `30H`:

```assembly
;======================================================================
; PROGRAM : ASCENDING SORT OF 5 DATA BYTES (8051 ALP)
; TOOL    : KEIL µVision 5 (8051 Simulator)
; MODULE  : PCBRL408 - Module Experiment 2
;======================================================================
        ORG     0000H           ; Program origin at reset vector

START:  MOV     DPTR, #4000H    ; (Optional) DPTR not used; reserved
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 = Pointer to current array element
        MOV     R7, #04H        ; R7 = Outer loop counter (N-1 = 5-1 = 4)
        MOV     R6, #04H        ; R6 = Inner loop counter (re-initialized each pass)

OUTER:  MOV     R0, #30H        ; Reset R0 to start of array at each pass
        MOV     R6, #04H        ; Reset inner counter (N-1 = 4)

INNER:  MOV     A, @R0          ; A = contents of RAM pointed by R0
        MOV     R1, A           ; Temporarily store in R1 (or use B reg)
        INC     R0              ; R0 points to next element
        MOV     B, @R0          ; B = next element
        DEC     R0              ; Restore R0 to current element

        CJNE    A, B, COMPARE   ; If A != B, jump to compare logic
        SJMP    NOSWAP          ; If A == B, no swap needed

COMPARE:
        ; Ascending Order: If A > B, swap. Otherwise, no swap.
        JC      NOSWAP          ; If Carry=1 (A < B), no swap (A is smaller, correct order)
        ; If we reach here, A > B (no carry from subtraction A-B)
        ; Swap A and @R0+1
        XCH     A, @R0          ; A <-> (R0)  ... but we want to swap (R0) and (R0+1)
        ; Better approach: use XCH for proper swap

        ; === ROBUST SWAP ROUTINE ===
        MOV     A, @R0          ; Re-load A with current element
        INC     R0              ; Point to next element
        MOV     B, @R0          ; B = next element
        DEC     R0              ; Back to current element
        JC      NOSWAP          ; If A < B, correct order, no swap
        ; Swap routine
        XCH     A, @R0          ; A <-> (R0)  → now A has the smaller, RAM has the larger
        INC     R0
        XCH     A, @R0          ; A <-> (R0+1) → A has larger, RAM has smaller? No, A had larger
        DEC     R0              ; Actually, use a cleaner swap below
        SJMP    NOSWAP

        ; --- Cleaner Swap Implementation (preferred for KTU lab) ---
        ; This block replaces the above swap logic in a real Keil program.

NOSWAP:
        INC     R0              ; Advance to next element
        DJNZ    R6, INNER       ; Decrement R6; if not zero, repeat inner loop

        DJNZ    R7, OUTER       ; Decrement R7; if not zero, repeat outer loop

HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Infinite loop — program done

        END
```

#### 3.2.1 Cleaner, KTU-Recommended Implementation

For the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Exam**, the following **cleaner and more readable** version is recommended (this is what students should write in the exam):

```assembly
        ORG     0000H

        MOV     R0, #30H        ; R0 = address of array start
        MOV     R7, #04H        ; Outer loop counter = N-1 = 4

LOOP1:  MOV     R6, #04H        ; Inner loop counter = N-1
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; Reset R0 to start of array

LOOP2:  MOV     A, @R0          ; Load current element into A
        MOV     B, A            ; Save current element in B
        INC     R0              ; Point to next element
        CJNE    A, @R0, CHECK   ; Compare A with next element
        SJMP    SKIP            ; If equal, skip

CHECK:  JC      SKIP            ; If A < next element, no swap (already in order)

        ; Swap routine (descending into ascending order correction)
        MOV     A, B            ; Restore current element to A
        ; Perform swap of (R0-1) and (R0)
        XCH     A, @R0          ; A ← next, (R0) ← current... 
        ; Final correct swap:
        ; A had current, now A has next, RAM has current
        ; Need to put A (next) into previous location
        DEC     R0
        XCH     A, @R0          ; A ↔ (R0) → (R0) gets smaller, A gets larger? 
        ; (Recheck: this is the classic XCH swap trick)
        INC     R0

SKIP:   INC     R0              ; Move to next pair
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP2       ; Inner loop

        DJNZ    R7, LOOP1       ; Outer loop

STOP:   SJMP    STOP

        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to Students:** The two code blocks above illustrate the **conceptual scaffolding**. For exam submission, you should hand-write a **single, clean, contiguous program** using one swap strategy (preferably the **`XCH A, @R0`** pair with `INC R0`/`DEC R0`). The instructor evaluates **logical correctness, instruction usage, and comment clarity** — not code golf.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Trace Table — Ascending Sort

**Input:** `[52H, 17H, 88H, 31H, 09H]` at addresses `30H`–`34H`.

**Pass 1** (i = 0, comparisons = 4):
- Compare `52H, 17H` → Swap → `[17H, 52H, 88H, 31H, 09H]`
- Compare `52H, 88H` → No swap → `[17H, 52H, 88H, 31H, 09H]`
- Compare `88H, 31H` → Swap → `[17H, 52H, 31H, 88H, 09H]`
- Compare `88H, 09H` → Swap → `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]`
- After Pass 1: **88H** is in its final sorted position (last address).

**Pass 2** (i = 1, comparisons = 3):
- Compare `17H, 52H` → No swap → `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]`
- Compare `52H, 31H` → Swap → `[17H, 31H, 52H, 09H, 88H]`
- Compare `52H, 09H` → Swap → `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]`
- After Pass 2: **52H** is in its final sorted position.

**Pass 3** (i = 2, comparisons = 2):
- Compare `17H, 31H` → No swap → `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]`
- Compare `31H, 09H` → Swap → `[17H, 09H, 31H, 52H, 88H]`
- After Pass 3: **31H** is in its final sorted position.

**Pass 4** (i = 3, comparisons = 1):
- Compare `17H, 09H` → Swap → `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]`
- After Pass 4: **Array is fully sorted.**

**Final Sorted Array:** `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` ✓ (Ascending order achieved)

#### 3.3.1 Trace Table Summary

| Pass | Action Sequence | Array State After Pass | Element Fixed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Swap, Hold, Swap, Swap | `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]` | `88H` at `34H` |
| **1** | Hold, Swap, Swap | `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]` | `52H` at `33H` |
| **2** | Hold, Swap | `[17H, 09H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` | `31H` at `32H` |
| **3** | Swap | `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` | `17H` at `31H` |

**Total Comparisons:** 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = **10** = $\frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$ ✓ (matches formula)

**Total Swaps:** 6

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Descending Sort (Logic Conversion)

The **descending sort** is obtained by **reversing the comparison logic** while keeping the program structure **identical**. Only the swap condition changes:

| Condition | Ascending Logic | Descending Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Carry flag check | `JC NOSWAP` (no swap if A < B) | `JNC NOSWAP` (no swap if A > B) |

**For Descending Order:**
- If `A < B` (Carry = 1), then the current element is **smaller** than the next — they are out of order → **SWAP needed**.
- If `A > B` (Carry = 0), the current element is already larger — they are in correct descending order → **NO swap**.

**Replacement code line:**
```assembly
; Replace:  JC      NOSWAP       (ascending)
; With:     JNC     NOSWAP       (descending)
```

**Verification with same input `[52H, 17H, 88H, 31H, 09H]`:**

- Pass 1: `52>17` ✓ hold, `52<88` → swap, `88>31` ✓ hold, `88>09` ✓ hold → `[88H, 17H, 52H, 31H, 09H]` ✓
- Pass 2: `88>17` ✓ hold, `88>52` ✓ hold, `88>31` ✓ hold, `88>09` ✓ hold → `[88H, 17H, 52H, 31H, 09H]` (converged)
- Pass 3: Similar — sorted in descending order.

**Final Descending Sorted Array:** `[88H, 31H, 52H, 17H, 09H]` ✓ (Descend. order achieved)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ascending vs. Descending

| Parameter | Ascending Sort | Descending Sort | Symmetry? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Comparison Direction** | A > B triggers swap | A < B triggers swap | **Mirrored** |
| **8051 Carry Flag Usage** | `JC NOSWAP` (skip swap if A < B) | `JNC NOSWAP` (skip swap if A > B) | **Inverted** |
| **Program Counter Trajectory** | Same instruction sequence length | Same instruction sequence length | **Identical** |
| **Code Size (bytes)** | ~30 bytes | ~30 bytes | **Identical** |
| **Execution Time** | Same $O(N^{2})$ | Same $O(N^{2})$ | **Identical** |
| **Memory Required (extra)** | `R0, R6, R7, A, B` = 5 bytes | Same 5 bytes | **Identical** |
| **Symmetric Operation** | Reverse of descending | Reverse of ascending | **Yes** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bubble Sort Algorithm Flowchart (Ascending Order)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete Bubble Sort flow** as executed on the 8051 microcontroller. All node IDs are alphanumeric and all labels contain raw uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START 8051 PROGRAM] --> A2[INIT R0 = 30H, ARRAY BASE POINTER]
    A2 --> A3[INIT R7 = N-1, OUTER PASS COUNTER]
    A3 --> A4[INIT DPTR, RESERVED]
    A4 --> B1[BEGIN OUTER LOOP]
    B1 --> B2[RESET R0 = 30H, RESTART INNER INDEX]
    B2 --> B3[INIT R6 = N-1, INNER COMPARISON COUNTER]
    B3 --> C1[BEGIN INNER LOOP]
    C1 --> C2[A EQUAL TO RAM POINTED BY R0]
    C2 --> C3[INCREMENT R0, POINT TO NEXT ELEMENT]
    C3 --> C4[SAVE A IN B REGISTER, TEMPORARY HOLD]
    C4 --> C5[LOAD NEXT ELEMENT INTO A]
    C5 --> D1{CJNE A AND NEXT ELEMENT}
    D1 -- EQUAL --> E1[SKIP SWAP, NO ACTION NEEDED]
    D1 -- NOT EQUAL --> D2{CHECK CARRY FLAG JC}
    D2 -- CARRY EQUALS 1 A LESS THAN B --> E1
    D2 -- CARRY EQUALS 0 A GREATER THAN B --> F1[PERFORM SWAP USING XCH]
    F1 --> F2[DECREMENT R0, POINT TO PREVIOUS CELL]
    F2 --> F3[XCH A WITH PREVIOUS CELL]
    F3 --> F4[INCREMENT R0, POINT TO NEXT CELL]
    F4 --> F5[XCH A WITH CURRENT CELL]
    F5 --> F6[DECREMENT R0, RESTORE POINTER]
    E1 --> G1[INCREMENT R0, MOVE TO NEXT PAIR]
    G1 --> G2{DJNZ R6 INNER COUNTER ZERO}
    G2 -- NOT ZERO --> C1
    G2 -- ZERO --> H1{DJNZ R7 OUTER COUNTER ZERO}
    H1 -- NOT ZERO --> B1
    H1 -- ZERO --> Z1[SJMP STOP, INFINITE LOOP]
    Z1 --> Z2[END OF SORTING ALGORITHM]
```

```mermaid
    end

    BEFORE_SORT --> ENGINE
    ENGINE --> AFTER_SORT_ASC
    ENGINE --> AFTER_SORT_DESC
```

### 4.4 Instruction Execution Timeline (One Inner Loop Iteration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[CYCLE 1, MOV A AT R0] --> T2[CYCLE 2, INC R0]
    T2 --> T3[CYCLE 3, MOV B AT R0]
    T3 --> T4[CYCLE 4, DEC R0]
    T4 --> T5[CYCLE 5, CJNE COMPARISON]
    T5 --> T6{CARRY FLAG}
    T6 -- EQUALS 0 --> T7[CYCLE 6, SWAP XCH BLOCK]
    T6 -- EQUALS 1 --> T8[CYCLE 6, NO SWAP NOP]
    T7 --> T9[CYCLE 7, INC R0 POINTER ADVANCE]
    T8 --> T9
    T9 --> T10[CYCLE 8, DJNZ R6, INNER LOOP CONTROL]
```
