## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** Differentiate between **ascending** and **descending** sorting. State the **swap condition** for each case in the Bubble Sort algorithm when implemented on the 8051 microcontroller.

**Model Answer:**

**Ascending Sort:** Rearranges array elements so that the **smallest value occupies the lowest memory address** and the **largest value occupies the highest memory address**.

**Descending Sort:** Rearranges array elements so that the **largest value occupies the lowest memory address** and the **smallest value occupies the highest memory address**.

**Bubble Sort Swap Conditions (8051 Implementation):**

| Sort Type | Swap Trigger | 8051 Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Ascending | If `A > @R0` (current element greater than next) | `JC NOSWAP` then swap |
| Descending | If `A < @R0` (current element smaller than next) | `JNC NOSWAP` then swap |

**(3 marks — 1 for definitions, 1 for ascending condition, 1 for descending condition)**

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** An 8051 microcontroller is used to sort an array of **8 unsigned bytes** stored at internal RAM locations `40H` to `47H`. Calculate the **(a)** total number of passes, **(b)** total number of comparisons in the worst case, and **(c)** the 8051 instruction used to perform an atomic byte swap in internal RAM.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $N = 8$ (array size), starting address = `40H`, end address = `40H + 7 = 47H`.

**(a) Total Passes:**
$$
P = N - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ passes}
$$

**(b) Total Comparisons (Worst Case):**
$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = \dfrac{8 \times 7}{2} = \dfrac{56}{2} = 28 \text{ comparisons}
$$

**(c) Atomic Swap Instruction:**
The `XCH A, @R0` instruction **atomically exchanges** the contents of the Accumulator with the internal RAM location pointed to by `R0`. It is a **1-byte, 1-machine-cycle instruction** (12 clock cycles).

**(3 marks — 1 for each sub-part)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** Write a complete **8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP)** to sort an array of **5 unsigned bytes** stored at internal RAM locations `30H` to `34H` in **ascending order** using the **Bubble Sort algorithm**. **(a)** Draw the flowchart and explain the algorithm in detail. **(b)** Show the **trace table** for the input array `[52H, 17H, 88H, 31H, 09H]` and verify the sorted output.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Algorithm Explanation:**
Bubble Sort works by **repeatedly comparing adjacent pairs** of elements and **swapping them if they are out of order**. After each complete pass through the array, the **largest unsorted element settles at the end** (in ascending sort). The algorithm requires **N − 1 passes** for an array of N elements.

**8051 Assembly Language Program:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; Pointer to array start
        MOV     R7, #04H        ; Outer counter = N-1 = 4

LOOP1:  MOV     R6, #04H        ; Inner counter = N-1
        MOV     R0, #30H        ; Reset array pointer each pass

LOOP2:  MOV     A, @R0          ; A = current element
        MOV     B, A            ; Save in B
        INC     R0              ; Point to next
        MOV     A, @R0          ; A = next element
        CJNE    A, B, LABEL1    ; Compare
        SJMP    SKIP

LABEL1: JC      SKIP            ; Ascending: if A<B (carry), no swap
        ; SWAP routine begins
        MOV     A, B            ; Restore smaller to A
        INC     R0              ; Point to larger element (B's location)
        MOV     @R0, A          ; Store smaller at higher address... 
        ; Better to use XCH-based swap:
        DEC     R0              ; Back to smaller's location
        XCH     A, @R0          ; Swap contents
        INC     R0
        XCH     A, @R0
        DEC     R0

SKIP:   INC     R0              ; Move to next pair
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP2       ; Inner loop control
        DJNZ    R7, LOOP1       ; Outer loop control

STOP:   SJMP    STOP            ; Endless loop

        END
```

**Flowchart** (refer to **Section 4.1** for the detailed Mermaid flowchart; the same logic applies here).

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Trace Table for input `[52H, 17H, 88H, 31H, 09H]`:**

| Pass | Comparisons | Swap? | Array After Each Comparison | Array After Pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | (52H, 17H) | Yes | `[17H, 52H, 88H, 31H, 09H]` | `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]` |
| | (52H, 88H) | No | `[17H, 52H, 88H, 31H, 09H]` | |
| | (88H, 31H) | Yes | `[17H, 52H, 31H, 88H, 09H]` | |
| | (88H, 09H) | Yes | `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]` | |
| **2** | (17H, 52H) | No | `[17H, 52H, 31H, 09H, 88H]` | `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]` |
| | (52H, 31H) | Yes | `[17H, 31H, 52H, 09H, 88H]` | |
| | (52H, 09H) | Yes | `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]` | |
| **3** | (17H, 31H) | No | `[17H, 31H, 09H, 52H, 88H]` | `[17H, 09H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` |
| | (31H, 09H) | Yes | `[17H, 09H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` | |
| **4** | (17H, 09H) | Yes | `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` | `[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]` |

**Verification:** After 4 passes, the array at `30H`–`34H` reads **[09H, 17H, 31H, 52H, 88H]** which is in **ascending order** ✓.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** Design a 8051 ALP to sort an array of **6 unsigned bytes** stored at internal RAM locations `40H` to `45H` in **descending order** using Bubble Sort. **(a)** Write the complete program with detailed comments and explain the role of the **Carry flag** in the comparison logic. **(b)** Calculate the **total number of comparisons and swaps** required for the worst case, and compute the **execution time** assuming a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0, #40H        ; Pointer to array base
        MOV     R7, #05H        ; Outer counter = N-1 = 5

LOOP1:  MOV     R6, #05H        ; Inner counter = N-1 = 5
        MOV     R0, #40H        ; Reset pointer each pass

LOOP2:  MOV     A, @R0          ; A = current element
        MOV     B, A            ; Save in B (temporary)
        INC     R0              ; Point to next element
        CJNE    A, @R0, LABEL1  ; Compare current with next
        SJMP    SKIP            ; If equal, skip

LABEL1: JNC     SKIP            ; DESCENDING: if A > next, no swap (correct order)
                                ; If carry = 1 (A < next), swap needed
        ; Swap routine (descending)
        XCH     A, @R0          ; Swap A with (R0)
        DEC     R0
        XCH     A, @R0          ; Complete the swap
        INC     R0

SKIP:   INC     R0              ; Advance pointer
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP2       ; Inner loop
        DJNZ    R7, LOOP1       ; Outer loop

DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

**Role of the Carry Flag:**

The **`CJNE A, @R0, label`** instruction performs an **internal subtraction** `A − @R0` **without modifying** the `A` register. The result of this subtraction is reflected in the **Carry Flag (CY, PSW.7)**:

- If `A ≥ @R0`, no borrow occurs → **CY = 0** → the subtraction result is non-negative.
- If `A < @R0`, a borrow occurs → **CY = 1** → the subtraction result is negative.

**For Ascending Sort:**
- We want to swap when `A > @R0` (out of order). Since the swap condition triggers when `A > @R0`, we use **`JC SKIP`** (skip the swap if `A < @R0`, indicated by Carry = 1).

**For Descending Sort:**
- We want to swap when `A < @R0` (out of order in descending). We use **`JNC SKIP`** (skip the swap if `A > @R0`, indicated by Carry = 0).

The Carry Flag is therefore the **deciding flag** that differentiates the two sort directions within the same code structure.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $N = 6$, Crystal frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

**Total Comparisons (Worst Case):**
$$
C_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15 \text{ comparisons}
$$

**Total Swaps (Worst Case — Reverse Sorted Input):**
For an input that is **already sorted in ascending order** (worst case for descending sort), every comparison triggers a swap:
$$
S_{\text{max}} = C_{\text{total}} = 15 \text{ swaps}
$$

**Clock Period Calculation:**
$$
T_{\text{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \dfrac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 90.42 \text{ ns}
$$

**Machine Cycle Period (8051 uses 12 clocks per machine cycle):**
$$
T_{\text{MC}} = 12 \times T_{\text{clock}} = 12 \times 90.42 \text{ ns} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

**Execution Time Calculation (Approximate):**

Each comparison involves roughly **5–7 instructions** (`MOV A,@R0` = 1 cycle, `MOV B,A` = 1 cycle, `INC R0` = 1 cycle, `CJNE` = 2 cycles, branch = 2 cycles, etc.). Assuming an average of **~7 machine cycles per inner loop iteration**:

$$
T_{\text{inner}} = 7 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 7.595 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total comparisons}} = 15 \times 7.595 \text{ µs} = 113.925 \text{ µs}
$$

**Total Execution Time (including swap overhead, ~5 machine cycles per swap):**
$$
T_{\text{swap}} = 15 \times 5 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 81.375 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{\text{grand total}} = T_{\text{comparisons}} + T_{\text{swaps}} = 113.925 + 81.375 = 195.3 \text{ µs}
$$

**Final Answer:** The worst-case execution time is approximately **195.3 µs**, which is negligible for most embedded applications but measurable on a high-speed oscilloscope.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCBRL408 Module 2:**
> 1. **Missing `ORG 0000H` directive** — Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if the program origin is not specified, as it indicates incomplete understanding of 8051 memory mapping.
> 2. **Confusion between `MOV A, R0` and `MOV A, @R0`** — This is the **single most common bug** in 8051 ALP. Always remember: `@` means **indirect addressing** (the cell pointed to by R0), not the contents of R0 itself.
> 3. **Incorrect loop counter initialization** — Students often set `R7 = N` instead of `R7 = N - 1`. This causes one **extra unnecessary pass**, which can corrupt the output or loop indefinitely.
> 4. **Not resetting `R0` at the start of each outer loop** — The pointer must be reset to the array base address at the **beginning of every outer loop iteration**, not just once at program start.
> 5. **Forgetting to initialize the carry flag** — Although `CJNE` automatically sets the carry flag, students sometimes manually clear it before the comparison, causing **erroneous branching logic**.
> 6. **Mismatched Jump Distance** — The `SJMP` instruction has a limited range of **−128 to +127 bytes** relative to the current PC. Long jumps require `LJMP` (3-byte instruction).
> 7. **Improper Swap Routine** — The XCH-based swap requires careful **DEC R0 / INC R0** sequencing; getting this wrong results in a **3-element rotation** instead of a 2-element swap.
> 8. **Lack of Comments in ALP** — KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires **commented code** in the lab record. Uncommented programs receive a **0.5 to 1 mark penalty**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — Pin These to Your Wall!**

- ✅ **Sorting** = rearrangement of array elements into ascending (small→large) or descending (large→small) order.
- ✅ **Bubble Sort** = adjacent comparison + conditional swap, repeated for **N − 1 passes**.
- ✅ **8051 Internal RAM** stores arrays from addresses `30H` to `7FH` (general-purpose region, avoiding register banks `00H–1FH` and bit-addressable area `20H–2FH`).
- ✅ **Ascending swap trigger:** `A > @R0` → use `JC NOSWAP` to skip the swap when `A < @R0`.
- ✅ **Descending swap trigger:** `A < @R0` → use `JNC NOSWAP` to skip the swap when `A > @R0`.
- ✅ **Carry Flag (CY / PSW.7)** is the **sole decision-maker** between ascending and descending logic.
- ✅ **Total Passes:** $P = N - 1$
- ✅ **Total Comparisons:** $C = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(N^{2})$; **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ (in-place sort).
- ✅ **Key 8051 instructions:** `MOV A,@R0` (1 byte, 1 cycle), `XCH A,@R0` (1 byte, 1 cycle), `CJNE A,direct,rel` (3 bytes, 2 cycles), `DJNZ Rn,label` (2 bytes, 2 cycles), `INC R0` (1 byte, 1 cycle).
- ✅ **Crystal frequency 11.0592 MHz** → 1 machine cycle = 1.085 µs → used for execution time calculations.
- ✅ **Register allocation convention:** `R0` = pointer, `R6` = inner counter, `R7` = outer counter, `A` = primary swap, `B` = secondary swap.
- ✅ **Keil µVision 5** with **Atmel AT89C51** target is the **mandatory simulation environment** per KTU 2024 Scheme.
- ✅ **Verification method:** Memory Window (D:0x30), Watch Window, breakpoint at `SJMP $`.
- ✅ **Stable sort:** Equal elements preserve their relative order (Bubble Sort is stable).
- ✅ **End-of-program marker:** `HERE: SJMP HERE` creates an infinite loop preventing "runaway" code execution.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Sorting (Ascending and Descending Order)**
> **Course:** PCBRL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB | **Scheme:** KTU 2024 | **Version:** V10.0
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL408 - MicroCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Matrix Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Addition** is a fundamental binary operation in linear algebra defined for two matrices of **identical order** $(m \times n)$, producing a resultant matrix of the **same order** whose each element is the algebraic sum of the corresponding elements of the operand matrices.

In the context of **PCBRL408 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, this experiment is implemented as an **embedded data-processing task** on a target microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051** family or **ARM Cortex-M**), where two pre-defined matrices are stored in **program memory (ROM/Flash)**, fetched sequentially into the **Accumulator/Register file**, added using the microcontroller's **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, and the result is stored either back in **internal RAM (XRAM/IRAM)** or displayed on an interfacing peripheral (LED array / LCD module / 7-segment display).

Mathematically, if $A = [a_{ij}]$ and $B = [b_{ij}]$ are two matrices of order $m \times n$, then their sum $C = A + B$ is defined as:

$$C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}, \quad \text{for } 1 \le i \le m, \quad 1 \le j \le n$$

The order constraint is **strict and non-negotiable** in both pure mathematics and embedded implementations: the microcontroller's address-generation logic (index pointer arithmetic) must operate on matrices of **fixed, pre-declared order**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Spreadsheet Column-Sum**

Imagine you are a class teacher maintaining **two separate mark registers** for the same set of 30 students in the subjects *Mathematics* and *Physics* — both registers arranged in identical rows (students) and columns (test scores). To compute a consolidated progress sheet, you walk down row-by-row, column-by-column, and **add the matching cell from Register A to the matching cell from Register B**, writing the result into Register C.

The **microcontroller performs exactly this chore**, but with two differences:
1. It never "walks" — it *loops* using indexed addressing.
2. It has no eyes — it must be **explicitly told** the matrix order, the base addresses, and the loop bound.

Think of the **Accumulator (`A` in 8051)** as your *mental scratchpad* where the teacher performs each individual addition before writing it down.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (8051-based Kit) | Typical Value (ARM Cortex-M) |
|---|---|---|
| Internal RAM | **128 Bytes (IRAM)** | **4 KB – 64 KB SRAM** |
| Program Memory | **4 KB – 64 KB ROM** | **16 KB – 512 KB Flash** |
| ALU Width | **8-bit** | **32-bit** |
| Clock Frequency | **11.0592 MHz / 12 MHz** | **Up to 180 MHz** |
| Addressing Modes Used | Indexed, Register Indirect | Base+Offset, Post-increment |

> [!NOTE]
> Because the **8051 ALU is 8-bit wide**, any matrix element requiring more than 8 bits (e.g., values > 255 unsigned, or signed arithmetic) demands a **multi-byte (2-byte) representation** and a corresponding **multi-precision addition algorithm** with carry propagation. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition (The "Why" and "How")

The matrix addition algorithm, when mapped onto a microcontroller, is a textbook example of **nested-loop control** with **indexed memory access**. The execution flow decomposes into the following logically separable stages:

### Stage 1 — Initialization Phase
- **Why:** Before the ALU touches any data, the **program counter**, the **index registers (R0/R1 in 8051, or R0–R12 in ARM)**, and the **loop counters** must be primed to known, deterministic values.
- **How:** Load the **base address** of Matrix A, the **base address** of Matrix B, and the **base address** of the result Matrix C into dedicated register pairs. Initialize outer loop counter (rows) and inner loop counter (columns).

### Stage 2 — Element Fetch Phase
- **Why:** The 8051's internal RAM is byte-addressable, but matrix elements may be **multi-byte**. The MCU must know precisely *where* to fetch.
- **How:** Use the **@R0 / @R1 indirect addressing mode** (or DPTR-based indexed mode) to read from the base address + offset.

### Stage 3 — Arithmetic Phase
- **Why:** The **ADD** instruction in 8051 places the result in the **Accumulator (A)** and sets the **Carry Flag (CY)** if an overflow occurs.
- **How:** Load first operand into `A`, add second operand using `ADD A, @R1` (or `ADDC` for multi-byte), producing one element of matrix C.

### Stage 4 — Storage Phase
- **Why:** The computed element must be written to the destination matrix in memory to be retrievable later.
- **How:** Store `A` into the corresponding location in Matrix C using `MOV @R0, A`.

### Stage 5 — Index Update & Loop Control
- **Why:** Pointers and counters must be incremented to advance to the next matrix cell.
- **How:** Increment source and destination pointers, decrement column counter; if columns > 0, repeat inner loop; else decrement row counter and reload column counter for outer loop.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Variables / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Matrix Sum Definition | $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}$ | $i \in [1, m], \; j \in [1, n]$ |
| 2 | Order Constraint | $\text{order}(A) = \text{order}(B) = \text{order}(C) = m \times n$ | $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ |
| 3 | Total Elements | $N = m \times n$ | Number of ALU operations |
| 4 | Linear Address Map (Row-Major) | $\text{addr}(A_{ij}) = \text{base}_A + (i \cdot n + j) \cdot s$ | $s = $ bytes per element |
| 5 | Inner Loop Counter (Cols) | $C_{\text{count}} = n$ | Decremented to 0 |
| 6 | Outer Loop Counter (Rows) | $R_{\text{count}} = m$ | Decremented to 0 |
| 7 | Multi-precision Add (8051) | $A_{\text{high}} \leftarrow A_{\text{high}} + B_{\text{high}} + CY_{\text{prev}}$ | $CY \in \{0,1\}$ |
| 8 | Carry Flag After 8-bit ADD | $CY = 1 \iff (A_{ij} + B_{ij}) > 255$ | Unsigned interpretation |
| 9 | Execution Cycles (8051) | $T_{\text{total}} = N \times (T_{\text{fetch}} + T_{\text{add}} + T_{\text{store}}) + T_{\text{overhead}}$ | $T_{\text{fetch}} = 12$ clock cycles |
| 10 | Pointer Increment Delta | $\Delta_{\text{ptr}} = s$ | Usually $s = 1$ or $2$ bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use $\text{addr}(\cdot)$ with parentheses, never $\vert \text{addr} \vert$ — KTU valuation scripts (and the LaTeX parser) reject raw pipe characters inside markdown tables. Use $\mid$ for *divides* notation if needed.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Matrix addition is **not a trivial academic exercise** in embedded systems. It is the computational heart of:

- **Image Processing Pipelines** — Adding two image frames (video mixing, alpha blending) on a DSP or microcontroller.
- **Sensor Fusion** — Combining accelerometer and gyroscope data matrices in an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**.
- **Control Systems** — State-space controllers where state matrices are added for superposition analysis.
- **Neural Network Inference** — Bias addition in fully-connected layers: $Z = W \cdot X + b$.
- **Audio Processing** — Mixing two PCM audio streams sample-by-sample (which is 1-D matrix addition).

Mastery of this experiment builds the foundation for **DCT transforms, convolution operations, and FIR filtering** in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: 2×2 Matrix Addition on 8051

Let us consider two concrete matrices to be summed on an 8051 kit:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0x12 & 0x34 \\ 0x56 & 0x78 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0x11 & 0x22 \\ 0x33 & 0x44 \end{bmatrix}$$

The expected result is:

$$C = A + B = \begin{bmatrix} 0x12 + 0x11 & 0x34 + 0x22 \\ 0x56 + 0x33 & 0x78 + 0x44 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0x23 & 0x56 \\ 0x89 & 0xBC \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.1 Element-by-Element Analytical Walkthrough

**Element $C_{11}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= A_{11} + B_{11} \\
       &= 0x12 + 0x11 \\
       &= 0x12_{(18)} + 0x11_{(17)} \\
       &= 0x23_{(35)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** 0x12 in hex = $1 \cdot 16 + 2 = 18$ in decimal; 0x11 = $1 \cdot 16 + 1 = 17$; sum = $18 + 17 = 35 = 2 \cdot 16 + 3 = 0x23$. Carry flag $CY = 0$ since $35 < 255$.

**Element $C_{12}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{12} &= A_{12} + B_{12} \\
       &= 0x34 + 0x22 \\
       &= 0x56
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** $0x34 = 52$, $0x22 = 34$, sum = $86 = 5 \cdot 16 + 6 = 0x56$. $CY = 0$.

**Element $C_{21}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{21} &= A_{21} + B_{21} \\
       &= 0x56 + 0x33 \\
       &= 0x89
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** $0x56 = 86$, $0x33 = 51$, sum = $137 = 8 \cdot 16 + 9 = 0x89$. $CY = 0$ (still under 255).

**Element $C_{22}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{22} &= A_{22} + B_{22} \\
       &= 0x78 + 0x44 \\
       &= 0xBC
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** $0x78 = 120$, $0x44 = 68$, sum = $188 = 11 \cdot 16 + 12 = 0xBC$. $CY = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If, instead, we had $0xFF + 0x01$, the sum would be $0x00$ with $CY = 1$. **The 8051 Carry Flag is NOT discarded;** in a multi-byte addition (16-bit, 32-bit operands), `ADDC` (Add with Carry) uses this flag to propagate to the higher-order byte. **Forgetting `ADDC` is a guaranteed 3-mark loss in KTU exams.**

## 3.2 Memory Map Derivation (8051 IRAM Layout)

For a 2×2 matrix of single-byte elements, we need $4$ bytes per matrix. A typical allocation:

| Memory Region | Address Range (Hex) | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| Matrix A (Source 1) | `0x30` – `0x33` | `0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78` |
| Matrix B (Source 2) | `0x40` – `0x43` | `0x11, 0x22, 0x33, 0x44` |
| Matrix C (Result)   | `0x50` – `0x53` | `0x23, 0x56, 0x89, 0xBC` (filled at runtime) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0x30 onwards?** The 8051's lower 32 bytes (0x00–0x1F) are the **Register Banks** and **Bit-Addressable Area**. Allocating matrices here causes **register-bank corruption** during interrupt servicing. Locations 0x30–0x7F are the safe, conventional user-RAM region in 8051.

## 3.3 Complete 8051 Assembly Algorithm (Annotated, Line-by-Line)

```asm
;=============================================================
; PROGRAM : 2x2 Matrix Addition (PCBRL408 - Module Exp 2)
; TARGET  : Intel 8051 / 8052
; ASSUMES : A[2x2] at 30H-33H, B[2x2] at 40H-43H, C[2x2] at 50H-53H
;=============================================================

        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H     ; R0 = Base address of Matrix A
        MOV     R1, #40H     ; R1 = Base address of Matrix B
        MOV     R2, #50H     ; R2 = Base address of Matrix C
        MOV     R3, #04H     ; R3 = Total elements counter (2x2 = 4)

LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0       ; A <- A[i]   (FETCH from Matrix A)
        ADD     A, @R1       ; A <- A[i] + B[i]   (ARITHMETIC)
        MOV     @R2, A       ; C[i] <- A   (STORE to Matrix C)
        INC     R0           ; Pointer to A -> next element
        INC     R1           ; Pointer to B -> next element
        INC     R2           ; Pointer to C -> next element
        DJNZ    R3, LOOP     ; Decrement counter; if not 0, repeat
HERE:   SJMP    HERE         ; Infinite loop: Halt / End of program
        END
```

### 3.3.1 Trace Table (Cycle-by-Cycle Verification)

Let us trace this program for the four elements to **prove correctness**:

| Cycle | R0 (Ptr A) | R1 (Ptr B) | R2 (Ptr C) | R3 (Counter) | A (Acc) | Memory @R2 Written | CY |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | `0x30` | `0x40` | `0x50` | `0x04` | `—` | — | — |
| Iter 1 | `0x30`→`0x31` | `0x40`→`0x41` | `0x50`→`0x51` | `0x04`→`0x03` | `0x23` | `0x50: 0x23` | 0 |
| Iter 2 | `0x31`→`0x32` | `0x41`→`0x42` | `0x51`→`0x52` | `0x03`→`0x02` | `0x56` | `0x51: 0x56` | 0 |
| Iter 3 | `0x32`→`0x33` | `0x42`→`0x43` | `0x52`→`0x53` | `0x02`→`0x01` | `0x89` | `0x52: 0x89` | 0 |
| Iter 4 | `0x33`→`0x34` | `0x43`→`0x44` | `0x53`→`0x54` | `0x01`→`0x00` | `0xBC` | `0x53: 0xBC` | 0 |
| Exit | `0x34` | `0x44` | `0x54` | `0x00` | `0xBC` | All written | 0 |

**Validation:** All four result cells match the expected $C$ matrix computed in Section 3.1. **Algorithm is correct.**

## 3.4 Embedded-C Equivalent (Keil / SDCC for 8051)

For completeness — the **same algorithm** expressed in Embedded C (commonly tested in KTU viva):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char code matrixA[4] = {0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78};  // 'code' = ROM
unsigned char code matrixB[4] = {0x11, 0x22, 0x33, 0x44};
unsigned char xdata matrixC[4];                              // 'xdata' = external RAM

void matrix_add_2x2(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
        matrixC[i] = matrixA[i] + matrixB[i];
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `code` keyword forces storage in **Flash/ROM** (non-volatile, where the kit's monitor program expects them), and `xdata` places the result in **external RAM** (visible to the user on memory-mapped displays). This separation of *immutable inputs* and *mutable outputs* is a key KTU viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following flowchart captures the **complete control flow** of the 8051 matrix addition algorithm, including initialization, inner-loop logic, and termination:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([START]) --> initR0["R0 = 0x30 (Base of Matrix A)"]
    initR0 --> initR1["R1 = 0x40 (Base of Matrix B)"]
    initR1 --> initR2["R2 = 0x50 (Base of Matrix C)"]
    initR2 --> initR3["R3 = 0x04 (Loop Counter = m*n)"]
    initR3 --> loopEntry[/"Loop Check: R3 ≠ 0"/]
    loopEntry -- Yes --> fetchA["A = MEM[R0]   (Fetch A[i])"]
    fetchA --> addOp["A = A + MEM[R1]   (ADD A, @R1)"]
    addOp --> storeC["MEM[R2] = A   (Store to C[i])"]
    storeC --> incR0["R0 = R0 + 1"]
    incR0 --> incR1["R1 = R1 + 1"]
    incR1 --> incR2["R2 = R2 + 1"]
    incR2 --> decR3["R3 = R3 - 1   (DJNZ)"]
    decR3 --> loopEntry
    loopEntry -- No --> halt1([HALT: SJMP HERE])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **hardware-software interaction** during matrix addition on a generic microcontroller kit (8051 / ARM):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph programMem["PROGRAM MEMORY (Flash / ROM)"]
        instA["Matrix A data (0x12, 0x34, 0x56, 0x78)"]
        instB["Matrix B data (0x11, 0x22, 0x33, 0x44)"]
        instCode["Instruction stream: MOV, ADD, DJNZ"]
    end

    subgraph cpuCore["CPU CORE"]
        pcReg["Program Counter PC"]
        accum["Accumulator A (8-bit)"]
        aluUnit["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
        idxReg["Index Registers R0, R1, R2"]
        cntReg["Counter Register R3"]
        cyFlag["Carry Flag CY"]
    end

    subgraph dataMem["DATA MEMORY (Internal RAM)"]
        memA["Region 0x30-0x33: Matrix A"]
        memB["Region 0x40-0x43: Matrix B"]
        memC["Region 0x50-0x53: Matrix C (Result)"]
    end

    subgraph outputIface["OUTPUT PERIPHERAL"]
        lcdDisp["16x2 LCD Module / LED Array"]
    end

    pcReg -- Fetch --> instCode
    instCode -- Decode --> idxReg
    idxReg -- Pointer --> memA
    idxReg -- Pointer --> memB
    memA -- Read --> accum
    memB -- Read --> aluUnit
    accum -- Operand A --> aluUnit
    aluUnit -- Sum --> accum
    accum -- Write --> memC
    cntReg -- Decrement --> idxReg
    aluUnit -- Overflow --> cyFlag
    memC -- Display --> lcdDisp
```

## 4.3 Register Allocation Diagram (8051 Register Bank 0)

| Register | Role | Initial Value | Final Value (After Loop) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R0 | Pointer to Matrix A | `0x30` | `0x34` (past-end) |
| R1 | Pointer to Matrix B | `0x40` | `0x44` (past-end) |
| R2 | Pointer to Matrix C | `0x50` | `0x54` (past-end) |
| R3 | Loop counter (N) | `0x04` | `0x00` |
| A (Acc) | Temporary result | — | `0xBC` (last sum) |
| PC | Program Counter | `0x0030` | `HERE: SJMP` |

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram (Iteration Timeline)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant MEM as Data Memory
    participant LCD as Output Display

    Note over CPU: INITIALIZE (R0, R1, R2, R3)
    loop For i = 1 to 4
        CPU->>MEM: READ A[i] from @R0
        MEM-->>CPU: returns A[i]
        CPU->>MEM: READ B[i] from @R1
        MEM-->>CPU: returns B[i]
        CPU->>CPU: ALU computes A[i] + B[i]
        CPU->>MEM: WRITE C[i] to @R2
        CPU->>CPU: R0++, R1++, R2++, R3--
    end
    CPU->>LCD: Display Matrix C contents
    LCD-->>CPU: ACK (optional)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define matrix addition. State and justify the **necessary and sufficient condition** under which two matrices can be added.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Matrix addition is a binary operation that produces a resultant matrix of the **same order** as the operands, with each element being the algebraic sum of the **corresponding elements** of the input matrices.

> **Condition (1 mark):** Both matrices must have **identical order** $m \times n$. Formally, if $A$ is of order $m_A \times n_A$ and $B$ is of order $m_B \times n_B$, then $A + B$ is defined **if and only if** $m_A = m_B$ and $n_A = n_B$. Otherwise, the operation is undefined because the element-to-element correspondence required by the definition cannot be established.

> **Justification (1 mark):** The definition $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}$ requires that for *every* index pair $(i,j)$ valid in $A$, an identical index pair $(i,j)$ exists in $B$ with the same bounds. Mismatched dimensions break this indexing invariant, leading to out-of-range memory accesses in a microcontroller implementation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** In an 8051-based matrix addition program, the instruction `ADD A, @R1` is used. Explain the **role of the Carry Flag (CY)** after this instruction, and describe what happens if the matrix elements are **16-bit** numbers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The `ADD A, @R1` instruction computes $A \leftarrow A + \text{MEM}[R1]$ and **updates the CY flag** to `1` if the result exceeds 8 bits (i.e., $A + \text{MEM}[R1] > 255$ in unsigned arithmetic); otherwise CY remains `0` **(1 mark)**.

> For **8-bit** elements, CY is typically ignored or used only for error detection. For **16-bit** elements stored as two consecutive bytes (low-byte, high-byte), the low bytes are added with `ADD` and the high bytes with `ADDC` (Add with Carry) **(1 mark)**, ensuring that any carry generated in the low-byte addition is propagated to the high-byte computation. Failing to use `ADDC` for the high byte would yield a **maximum error of 255** per element — a critical correctness bug **(1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q:** Design and write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to add two **3×3 matrices** stored in internal RAM starting at locations `0x30` (Matrix A) and `0x40` (Matrix B), storing the result in `0x50` (Matrix C). Explain your algorithm, draw the flowchart, and verify the result for the sample input:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0x10 & 0x20 & 0x30 \\ 0x40 & 0x50 & 0x60 \\ 0x70 & 0x80 & 0x90 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0x01 & 0x02 & 0x03 \\ 0x04 & 0x05 & 0x06 \\ 0x07 & 0x08 & 0x09 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (a) — Algorithm, Flowchart & Register Allocation [7 Marks]

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Initialize R0 = `0x30` (base of A), R1 = `0x40` (base of B), R2 = `0x50` (base of C), R3 = `0x09` (9 elements for 3×3).
2. **Loop Start:** Load A[i] from @R0 into Accumulator.
3. Add B[i] from @R1 to Accumulator using `ADD A, @R1`.
4. Store result into C[i] using `MOV @R2, A`.
5. Increment R0, R1, R2 by 1.
6. Decrement R3; if not zero, jump to Loop Start.
7. Stop.

**Flowchart (2 marks):** [See Section 4.1 above — adapt N = 9 and base addresses per question]

**Register Allocation Table (2 marks):**

| Register | Purpose | Initial Value |
|---|---|---|
| R0 | Source pointer (A) | `0x30` |
| R1 | Source pointer (B) | `0x40` |
| R2 | Destination pointer (C) | `0x50` |
| R3 | Loop counter | `0x09` |
| A | ALU operand / result | — |

#### Part (b) — Complete Assembly Code & Verification [7 Marks]

**Assembly Code (4 marks):**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #30H     ; Pointer to Matrix A
        MOV     R1, #40H     ; Pointer to Matrix B
        MOV     R2, #50H     ; Pointer to Matrix C
        MOV     R3, #09H     ; Counter = 3*3 = 9

LOOP:
        MOV     A, @R0       ; Fetch A[i]
        ADD     A, @R1       ; A = A[i] + B[i]
        MOV     @R2, A       ; Store C[i]
        INC     R0
        INC     R1
        INC     R2
        DJNZ    R3, LOOP
DONE:   SJMP    DONE
        END
```

**Verification (3 marks):**

$$C = A + B = \begin{bmatrix} 0x11 & 0x22 & 0x33 \\ 0x44 & 0x55 & 0x66 \\ 0x77 & 0x88 & 0x99 \end{bmatrix}$$

Trace: $C_{11} = 0x10 + 0x01 = 0x11$; $C_{12} = 0x20 + 0x02 = 0x22$; ... ; $C_{33} = 0x90 + 0x09 = 0x99$. All carries `CY = 0` since each sum $\le 0x99 < 0xFF$. The trace cycle-by-cycle analogous to Section 3.3.1 holds.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q:** Write an **8051 Embedded-C program** (Keil syntax) to perform matrix addition of two **2×3 matrices**. Use the `code` and `xdata` memory qualifiers appropriately. Explain the role of each memory class. Trace the output for the input:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0x05 & 0x0A & 0x0F \\ 0x14 & 0x19 & 0x1E \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0x01 & 0x02 & 0x03 \\ 0x04 & 0x05 & 0x06 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (a) — Program Design with Memory Classes [7 Marks]

**Embedded-C Code (5 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

#define ROWS 2
#define COLS 3
#define TOTAL (ROWS * COLS)

unsigned char code matrixA[TOTAL] = {
    0x05, 0x0A, 0x0F,     // Row 0 of A
    0x14, 0x19, 0x1E      // Row 1 of A
};

unsigned char code matrixB[TOTAL] = {
    0x01, 0x02, 0x03,     // Row 0 of B
    0x04, 0x05, 0x06      // Row 1 of B
};

unsigned char xdata matrixC[TOTAL];   // Result in external RAM

void matrix_add(void) {
    unsigned char idx;
    for (idx = 0; idx < TOTAL; idx++) {
        matrixC[idx] = matrixA[idx] + matrixB[idx];
    }
}

void main(void) {
    matrix_add();
    while (1);   // Halt here
}
```

**Memory Class Explanation (2 marks):**

- **`code`:** Forces the array to reside in **Flash/ROM** (program memory space). Used for input matrices whose values are **constants burned into the firmware** at programming time. The `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction is used internally to fetch such data.
- **`xdata`:** Allocates the result array in **external data memory** (XRAM, up to 64 KB on standard 8051). Used for the result so it is **writable at runtime** and can be easily inspected via the kit's memory-view window or dumped to a peripheral.

#### Part (b) — Output Trace & Validation [7 Marks]

**Computation Trace (4 marks):**

| idx | `matrixA[idx]` | `matrixB[idx]` | Hex Sum | Decimal Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x05` | `0x01` | `0x06` | 5+1 = 6 ✓ |
| 1 | `0x0A` | `0x02` | `0x0C` | 10+2 = 12 ✓ |
| 2 | `0x0F` | `0x03` | `0x12` | 15+3 = 18 ✓ |
| 3 | `0x14` | `0x04` | `0x18` | 20+4 = 24 ✓ |
| 4 | `0x19` | `0x05` | `0x1E` | 25+5 = 30 ✓ |
| 5 | `0x1E` | `0x06` | `0x24` | 30+6 = 36 ✓ |

**Resultant Matrix in Row-Major Form (2 marks):**

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 0x06 & 0x0C & 0x12 \\ 0x18 & 0x1E & 0x24 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Status of Carry Flag (1 mark):** `CY = 0` for all six operations since each sum is below `0xFF`. If any element of A or B exceeded `0xFC`, the corresponding `CY` would be set, but no `ADDC` is needed here because the operands are single-byte.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC` for multi-byte operands** → causes silent overflow errors. Maximum 3-mark penalty.
> 2. **Allocating matrices in the Register Bank area (0x00–0x1F)** → causes corruption when interrupts switch banks. 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting to initialize R3 (loop counter)** → infinite loop or single iteration only. 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Storing input matrices in `xdata` instead of `code`** → wastes writable RAM and violates best practice for constants. 1-mark penalty in viva.
> 5. **Confusing row-major vs column-major addressing** → answers "incorrect memory map" question, even when code runs. Often the deciding 1-mark difference between S and A grade.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Matrix addition is **defined only for matrices of identical order** $m \times n$.
- ✅ Element-wise: $C_{ij} = A_{ij} + B_{ij}$; no cross-multiplication is involved.
- ✅ In 8051, the **Accumulator (A)** is the implicit operand and result register of `ADD`.
- ✅ For **multi-byte operands**, always use `ADDC` (Add with Carry) for higher-order bytes.
- ✅ **Carry Flag (CY)** must be cleared (`CLR C`) before the first byte of a multi-precision addition; otherwise, stale carry from a prior operation corrupts the result.
- ✅ Typical 8051 RAM allocation: matrices at **`0x30+`** to avoid clashing with Register Banks 0–3 (0x00–0x1F) and Bit-Addressable RAM (0x20–0x2F).
- ✅ Loop counter `R3` holds the **total element count** $N = m \times n$, eliminating the need for a nested loop in 1-D traversal (the linear addressing collapses the 2-D structure).
- ✅ `DJNZ Rn, label` is the **preferred 8051 idiom** for loop control — it decrements and branches in a single instruction (2 machine cycles).
- ✅ In Embedded-C, use `code` qualifier for **constant** input data (Flash) and `xdata` for **runtime-writable** results (external RAM).
- ✅ KTU viva favourite: *"What happens if matrices have different orders?"* — Answer: operation undefined; in code, it would cause out-of-bounds memory reads producing garbage output.
- ✅ KTU viva favourite: *"Why use indexed addressing via R0/R1 instead of direct addresses?"* — Answer: because the offset varies per iteration; direct addresses are compile-time constants and cannot be looped.
- ✅ KTU viva favourite: *"How would you extend this to 16-bit elements?"* — Answer: increment pointers by 2 instead of 1; use `ADDC` for the high byte; double the loop count to $2N$ for byte-level iteration, OR use a higher-level abstraction (struct with two bytes).
- ✅ **Total number of machine cycles** for the full 3×3 program ≈ $(9 \times 9) + 5 \approx 86$ cycles ≈ $86 \times 12 / 11.0592\text{ MHz} \approx 93\ \mu\text{s}$ at 11.0592 MHz crystal.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Largest and Smallest Number in an Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Largest and Smallest Number in an Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **8051 Microcontroller Programming** (the standard target architecture for KTU PCBRL408), finding the **Largest** and **Smallest** number in an array is a fundamental *sequential search* operation stored in the internal RAM (idata) or code memory (code) of the microcontroller. The algorithm traverses an $N$-element array $A[0], A[1], \dots, A[N-1]$ using a loop, maintaining two accumulator/working variables: `MAX` and `MIN`. For every element $A[i]$, a conditional compare-and-swap operation is performed using the **JC (Jump if Carry)**, **JNC (Jump if No Carry)**, or in C, the simple `if-else` construct against the current extrema.

Mathematically, the algorithm computes:

$$MAX = \max_{0 \le i \le N-1}\{A[i]\}$$

$$MIN = \min_{0 \le i \le N-1}\{A[i]\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCBRL408):** This experiment directly maps to **CO2 – "Develop Assembly and Embedded C programs for 8051 to perform data manipulation and arithmetic operations."** The student is expected to implement the logic in **both Keil C51 and 8051 Assembly** and verify the output in the **Simulator (Proteus/Keil µVision Debugger)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **class teacher** with a stack of 50 answer sheets. You want to find the **highest scorer** and the **lowest scorer**.

- You pick the **first sheet** and place it on your desk as a reference.
- You then pick the **second sheet**. If the new mark is **greater** than the one on the desk, you **replace** it. If it is **smaller**, you keep the old one.
- You repeat this process for **all 50 sheets**.

The one left on your desk at the end is the **highest (or lowest)** — this is *exactly* what the microcontroller does inside its CPU using registers and memory pointers. The "desk" is the **accumulator or a working register (R0–R7)**, and the "stack of answer sheets" is the **array in memory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real embedded systems, this operation is the heart of:
> - **Peak Detection** in ECG / EEG biomedical signals.
> - **Sensor Thresholding** in fire alarms (LDR + thermistor arrays).
> - **ADC Sorting** in multi-channel data loggers.
> - **Statistical Pre-processing** in DSP (Digital Signal Processing) chips.

## 1.3 Key Embedded Constants & Metrics

- **DATA Memory size (8051):** **128 bytes** internal RAM (00H–7FH).
- **Register Banks:** **4 banks** of 8 registers each (R0–R7), selectable via PSW.3 and PSW.4.
- **Cycle Count (per comparison):** **1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator cycles** (standard 8051).
- **Typical instruction used:** `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) — a 2-byte, 2-cycle instruction.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(N)$ — Linear in the number of array elements.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **size of the array ($N$)**, the **starting address** (e.g., `30H` in internal RAM), and the **number of machine cycles** consumed when writing your algorithm. Examiners award grace marks for this completeness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The algorithm is a **single-pass linear scan** and works on the principle of *Invariant Maintenance*. The two core invariants are:

1. **MAX Invariant:** After processing the first $k$ elements, `MAX` holds the largest value among $A[0] \dots A[k-1]$.
2. **MIN Invariant:** After processing the first $k$ elements, `MIN` holds the smallest value among $A[0] \dots A[k-1]$.

### Step-by-Step Algorithmic Logic

- **Step 1 — Initialization (Why?):** Load the **first element** of the array into both `MAX` and `MIN`. This is the *base case*; it avoids using sentinel values like `0x00` or `0xFF` which would fail for arrays containing only negative or boundary numbers.
- **Step 2 — Pointer Setup (How?):** Initialize a loop counter (in 8051: register `R0` or `R1` acting as the index pointer) to point at the **second element** (index = 1).
- **Step 3 — Iteration & Compare (Why?):** For each new element $A[i]$:
  - Perform `CJNE A, MAX, label` to compare. If `A > MAX`, update `MAX`.
  - Perform `CJNE A, MIN, label` to compare. If `A < MIN`, update `MIN`.
- **Step 4 — Increment & Loop Control (How?):** Increment the index pointer, decrement a counter, and loop back until the array is exhausted.
- **Step 5 — Result Storage:** Store the final `MAX` and `MIN` into designated memory locations or display via **P1 / P2 / P3 port** or send to **serial UART (SBUF)**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula / 8051 Construct | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Comparisons | $2(N-1)$ | Two compares per element after the first |
| Total Comparisons (worst case) | $2N - 2$ | Linear scan; $O(N)$ |
| Total Comparisons (best case) | $2N - 2$ | Even with sorted input (no early exit optimization) |
| Time Complexity | $T(N) = c_1 + c_2 N$ | where $c_1$ = init cost, $c_2$ = per-compare cost |
| Machine Cycles per Compare (`CJNE`) | **2 cycles** | 8051 standard |
| Memory per Element | **1 byte** | 8-bit 8051 ALU width |
| Array Base Address (e.g.) | `0x30` | Internal RAM (idata) — must be $\ge$ 0x30 to avoid register bank area |
| Register used for pointer | `R0` or `R1` | Indirect addressing mode `@R0` |
| Loop counter register | `R2` or `R7` | Decremented each iteration |
| MAX/MIN storage registers | `R3` (MAX), `R4` (MIN) | Or any unused register from active bank |
| Carry flag behavior on `CJNE` | `CY = 1$ if $A <$ operand, else $0$` | Standard 8051 flag semantics |
| Total execution time | $T_{exec} = (2N-2) \times 2 \times T_{osc} \times 12$ | For pure compare path only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Initializing `MAX = 0x00` and `MIN = 0xFF`. This **fails** for arrays containing only negative or saturated values. **Always seed** `MAX` and `MIN` with `A[0]`.

## 2.3 Real-World Embedded Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Control:** PLCs often need to find peak temperature from 100 thermocouples — same algorithm.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Airbag controllers analyze accelerometer data streams to find peak G-force within a sliding window.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Sorting sensor peaks locally before transmitting to cloud (reduces bandwidth).
- **DSP Pre-Processing:** Computing the dynamic range $D = MAX - MIN$ is critical for normalization in audio codecs (MP3, AAC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Algorithm Walkthrough — Trace Table

Consider the array $A[5] = \{45, 12, 89, 23, 67\}$ stored at internal RAM starting address `0x30`.

| Step | Index $i$ | Element $A[i]$ | Current MAX | MAX Updated? | Current MIN | MIN Updated? | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| Init | — | — | 45 | — | 45 | — | Seed from $A[0]$ |
| 1 | 1 | 12 | 45 | No (45 > 12) | 12 | Yes (12 < 45) | MIN updated |
| 2 | 2 | 89 | 89 | Yes (89 > 45) | 12 | No | MAX updated |
| 3 | 3 | 23 | 89 | No | 12 | No | No change |
| 4 | 4 | 67 | 89 | No | 12 | No | No change |
| Final | — | — | **89** | — | **12** | — | Output |

**Result:** $MAX = 89$, $MIN = 12$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Cycle Count Calculation

Let $N = 5$. Each iteration involves:

- **2** `CJNE` instructions (one for MAX, one for MIN) $\Rightarrow 2 \times 2 = 4$ machine cycles.
- **1** `INC` (increment pointer) $\Rightarrow 1$ machine cycle.
- **1** `DJNZ` (decrement counter, jump if not zero) $\Rightarrow 2$ machine cycles.
- **1** pointer increment (array index) $\Rightarrow 1$ machine cycle.

Total per iteration $= 4 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 8$ cycles.

For $N - 1 = 4$ iterations (after the seeding step):

$$T_{loop} = (N-1) \times 8 = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ machine cycles}$$

Adding the initialization cost $C_{init} \approx 6$ cycles:

$$T_{total} = C_{init} + T_{loop} = 6 + 32 = 38 \text{ machine cycles}$$

Converting to time at a 12 MHz oscillator ($T_{osc} = 1/12\,\mu s$):

$$T_{exec} = 38 \times 12 \times T_{osc} = 38 \times 12 \times \frac{1}{12\,\mu s} = 38\,\mu s$$

## 3.3 Two Reference Implementations

### 3.3.1 Keil Embedded C (8051 Target)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char data arr[5] _at_ 0x30;  // Array at internal RAM 0x30
unsigned char data max_val _at_ 0x40;
unsigned char data min_val _at_ 0x41;

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    max_val = arr[0];   // Seed MAX with A[0]
    min_val = arr[0];   // Seed MIN with A[0]

    for (i = 1; i < 5; i++) {
        if (arr[i] > max_val)
            max_val = arr[i];      // Update MAX
        if (arr[i] < min_val)
            min_val = arr[i];      // Update MIN
    }

    while (1);  // Halt here for debugger inspection
}
```

### 3.3.2 8051 Assembly Language (`.asm`)

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
ARR:    DB      45, 12, 89, 23, 67   ; The array

        ORG     0050H
MAIN:
        MOV     DPTR, #ARR         ; DPTR points to array base
        MOVX    A, @DPTR           ; A = ARR[0]
        MOV     R3, A              ; R3 = MAX (seeded)
        MOV     R4, A              ; R4 = MIN (seeded)
        MOV     R0, #01H           ; Index = 1
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = N = 5

LOOP:
        MOV     A, R0
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR         ; A = ARR[i] (code-memory read)

        ; --- MAX compare ---
        CJNE    A, 03H, SKIP_MAX   ; A vs R3
SKIP_MAX:
        JC      SKIP_MIN           ; If A < R3, skip MAX update
        MOV     R3, A              ; MAX = A

SKIP_MIN:
        ; --- MIN compare ---
        CJNE    A, 04H, SKIP_MIN2  ; A vs R4
SKIP_MIN2:
        JNC     NEXT_ITER          ; If A >= R4, no MIN update
        MOV     R4, A              ; MIN = A

NEXT_ITER:
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP

        ; Store results in RAM 0x40 and 0x41
        MOV     40H, R3
        MOV     41H, R4

HERE:   SJMP    HERE               ; Infinite halt
        END
```

## 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Correctness (KTU Style)

**Claim:** After the loop completes, $R3 = \max A[i]$ and $R4 = \min A[i]$.

**Proof by Invariant Induction:**

- **Base Case ($k=1$):** After initialization, $R3 = R4 = A[0]$. The set of considered elements is $\{A[0]\}$, so $\max = \min = A[0]$. Invariant holds.

- **Inductive Step:** Assume after iteration $k-1$, $R3 = \max\{A[0]\dots A[k-2]\}$ and $R4 = \min\{A[0]\dots A[k-2]\}$. At iteration $k$, we compare $A[k-1]$:
  - If $A[k-1] > R3$, then $R3 \leftarrow A[k-1]$, and the new $R3$ is indeed $\max\{A[0]\dots A[k-1]\}$.
  - If $A[k-1] \le R3$, $R3$ remains the maximum.
  - Symmetric argument for $R4$.

- **Termination ($k = N$):** All $N$ elements processed, $R3 = \max A$, $R4 = \min A$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start]) --> nodeB[Initialize: i = 0]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Load A into MAX register]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Load A into MIN register]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Set i = 1, counter = N]
    nodeE --> nodeF{i less than N?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeO([Output MAX and MIN])
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Fetch A from index i]
    nodeG --> nodeH{A greater than MAX?}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[MAX = A]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Keep MAX unchanged]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK{A less than MIN?}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[MIN = A]
    nodeK -->|No| nodeM[Keep MIN unchanged]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Increment i, decrement counter]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeF
    nodeO([End])
```

## 4.2 8051 Internal RAM Memory Map for This Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR0[00H to 1FH: Register Banks R0 to R7]
    nodeR1[20H to 2FH: Bit Addressable Area]
    nodeR2[30H to 34H: Array A 5 bytes]
    nodeR3[35H: Scratch Byte]
    nodeR4[40H: MAX Result]
    nodeR5[41H: MIN Result]
    nodeR6[7FH: Top of internal RAM]
    nodeR0 --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4
    nodeR4 --> nodeR5
    nodeR5 --> nodeR6
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Hardware View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[8051 CPU Core]
    nodeS2[Program Memory code 4KB]
    nodeS3[Internal RAM 128B]
    nodeS4[ALU 8 bit]
    nodeS5[PSW Flags CY AC OV]
    nodeS6[Port P1 Output]
    nodeS7[Keil uVision Debugger]

    nodeS2 -->|Fetch ARR i| nodeS1
    nodeS1 -->|Indirect via R0| nodeS3
    nodeS1 -->|Compare via CJNE| nodeS4
    nodeS4 -->|Update CY flag| nodeS5
    nodeS5 -->|Conditional Jump JC JNC| nodeS1
    nodeS1 -->|Write final MAX MIN| nodeS3
    nodeS3 -->|Display via MOV P1| nodeS6
    nodeS1 -.->|Step run watch window| nodeS7
```

## 4.4 Register & Pointer Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    regR0[Register R0: Array Index Pointer]
    regR2[Register R2: Loop Counter N]
    regR3[Register R3: MAX Holder]
    regR4[Register R4: MIN Holder]
    regA[Accumulator A: Temp Compare Buffer]
    regDPTR[DPTR: Base Address Pointer]
    opFetch[Operation: MOVC A at A plus DPTR]
    opCmpMax[Operation: CJNE A R3]
    opCmpMin[Operation: CJNE A R4]

    regR0 --> opFetch
    regDPTR --> opFetch
    opFetch --> regA
    regA --> opCmpMax
    regR3 --> opCmpMax
    opCmpMax --> regR3
    regA --> opCmpMin
    regR4 --> opCmpMin
    opCmpMin --> regR4
    regR2 --> ctrlLoop[Loop Control DJNZ]
    ctrlLoop --> regR0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The `R0` register holds the **current index** of the array, `DPTR` holds the **base address**, and the `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` instruction fetches the element at `DPTR + index`. The accumulator `A` is then compared against `R3` (MAX) and `R4` (MIN) using `CJNE`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Why must `MAX` and `MIN` be initialized with `A[0]` rather than with `0x00` and `0xFF` in an 8051 program?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Initializing `MAX = 0x00` and `MIN = 0xFF` assumes the array contains only values in the range `[0x00, 0xFF]`, which is true for unsigned bytes. However, for **signed data** or arrays where the true extremum is at the boundary (e.g., a temperature array containing only `0x00` values), the algorithm would **incorrectly return wrong results**. Seeding both `MAX` and `MIN` with the first element `A[0]` is a *safe and general* approach that works for any data type and any value range. It guarantees that the invariants `MAX >= A[i]` and `MIN <= A[i]` hold from the very first comparison, **eliminating one comparison step** (the first iteration becomes a no-op) and **preventing logical errors** in edge cases. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**What is the time complexity of finding the largest element in an array of $N$ elements using a linear scan? Justify with the instruction count for an 8051 system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The time complexity is **$O(N)$** — linear in the number of elements. For an 8051 system, each comparison uses the `CJNE` instruction (2 machine cycles = 24 oscillator cycles). The total number of comparisons is $(N-1)$. Thus, the total machine cycles = $2(N-1)$ and the execution time is:

$$T_{exec} = 2(N-1) \times 12 \times T_{osc}$$

This linear relationship between $T_{exec}$ and $N$ confirms the **$O(N)$** complexity. Even if the array is sorted, this naive algorithm still performs $N-1$ comparisons — there is no early-exit optimization. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Write an **8051 Embedded C program** using **Keil µVision** to find the largest and smallest number in an array of **10 unsigned bytes** stored in internal RAM starting at address `0x30`. Store the results at `0x45H` and `0x46H`. Explain the seeding logic in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the above C program into an equivalent **8051 Assembly Language** program. Show the use of `CJNE`, `JC`, and `DJNZ` instructions. Calculate the **total machine cycles** consumed assuming a 12 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char data arr[10] _at_ 0x30;
unsigned char data max_result _at_ 0x45;
unsigned char data min_result _at_ 0x46;

void main(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    max_result = arr[0];  // Seed with first element — avoids boundary bugs
    min_result = arr[0];

    for (i = 1; i < 10; i++) {
        if (arr[i] > max_result)
            max_result = arr[i];
        if (arr[i] < min_result)
            min_result = arr[i];
    }

    while (1);  // Trap for debugger
}
```

**Seeding Logic Explanation (for 7-mark breakup):**
- *Initialization correctness:* Both `MAX` and `MIN` start with `arr[0]` (the first element) — this is the *only safe* initial value because it is guaranteed to belong to the dataset.
- *Why not 0x00/0xFF?* Such sentinel values assume the data lies strictly within (0, 255). For signed 2's-complement data or arrays containing boundary values, the algorithm would produce wrong output.
- *Loop boundary:* The loop starts at `i = 1` because the first element has already been used as the seed. The condition `i < 10` ensures all 10 elements are processed.
- *Result storage:* Final `MAX` is stored at internal RAM `0x45` and `MIN` at `0x46` as required.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
ARR:    DB      55, 22, 88, 11, 66, 33, 99, 44, 77, 10
        ORG     0100H
MAIN:
        MOV     DPTR, #ARR
        CLR     A
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR       ; A = ARR[0]
        MOV     R3, A            ; R3 = MAX seed
        MOV     R4, A            ; R4 = MIN seed
        MOV     R0, #01H         ; Index i = 1
        MOV     R2, #0AH         ; Counter = 10

LOOP:
        MOV     A, R0
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR       ; A = ARR[i]

        CJNE    A, AR3, CHECK_MAX
CHECK_MAX:
        JC      CHECK_MIN        ; If A < R3, jump to MIN check
        MOV     R3, A            ; MAX updated

CHECK_MIN:
        CJNE    A, AR4, NEXT
NEXT:
        JNC     INCR             ; If A >= R4, no update
        MOV     R4, A            ; MIN updated

INCR:
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP

        MOV     45H, R3
        MOV     46H, R4
HALT:   SJMP    HALT
        END
```

**Machine Cycle Calculation (7-mark breakdown):**

For each of the $(N-1) = 9$ iterations:
- `MOV A, R0` — 1 cycle
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — 2 cycles
- `CJNE A, R3, ...` — 2 cycles
- `JC` — 2 cycles
- `CJNE A, R4, ...` — 2 cycles
- `JNC` — 2 cycles
- `INC R0` — 1 cycle
- `DJNZ R2, LOOP` — 2 cycles

Per iteration $= 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 14$ cycles.

Total loop cycles $= 9 \times 14 = 126$ cycles.

Initialization overhead $\approx 10$ cycles. Final store $\approx 6$ cycles.

$$T_{total} = 10 + 126 + 6 = 142 \text{ machine cycles}$$

At 12 MHz, $T_{osc} = 1/12\,\mu s$, so each machine cycle = $12 \times T_{osc} = 1\,\mu s$.

$$T_{exec} = 142 \times 1\,\mu s = 142\,\mu s$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the algorithm to find the largest and smallest number in an array. Discuss the difference between **sorting the array first** and **single-pass scanning** in terms of time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an 8051 program to find the **largest of 5 signed numbers** stored at internal RAM location `0x40H` onwards. Discuss how the sign bit (bit 7) of an 8051 byte affects the comparison logic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Flowchart:** Refer to the flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes. (Award 3 marks for the flowchart, 4 marks for the complexity analysis.)

**Single-pass scanning complexity:**

$$T_{scan}(N) = 2(N-1) \text{ comparisons} = O(N)$$

**Sort-then-pick complexity:** A typical sort like Bubble Sort takes:

$$T_{sort}(N) = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \text{ comparisons} = O(N^2)$$

After sorting, picking the first and last element is $O(1)$. Total = $O(N^2) + O(1) = O(N^2)$.

**Conclusion:** Sorting first is **wasteful** for finding just the extrema. A single linear scan is asymptotically optimal and uses **$O(1)$ extra space** (just two registers). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

For signed numbers in 8051, **bit 7 is the sign bit** (0 = positive, 1 = negative). The standard `CJNE` instruction performs an **unsigned comparison** based on the Carry flag.

**Problem with unsigned compare for signed data:**
Consider two 8-bit signed numbers: `-5 = 0xFB` and `+10 = 0x0A`.
Unsigned compare: `0xFB (251) > 0x0A (10)` — but signed-wise, `-5 < +10`. **Wrong result!**

**Solution — Signed Compare Subroutine:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0040H
ARR_S:  DB      0FBH, 0AH, 07H, 80H, 0F0H   ; -5, +10, +7, -128, -16
        ORG     0080H
MAIN:
        MOV     R0, #40H          ; Pointer to array
        MOV     R2, #05H          ; Counter
        MOV     A, @R0            ; Load first element
        MOV     R3, A             ; MAX seed (signed)

LOOP_S:
        INC     R0
        MOV     A, @R0            ; Load next element
        ACALL   SCMP              ; Signed compare: A vs R3
        JZ      SKIP_S            ; Equal — skip
        JB      F0, LESS_S        ; F0 = 1 if A < R3
        MOV     R3, A             ; A > R3 → update MAX
        SJMP    SKIP_S
LESS_S: NOP                       ; A < R3 → no update
SKIP_S: DJNZ   R2, LOOP_S
        MOV     50H, R3           ; Store signed MAX
HALT:   SJMP    HALT

SCMP:   ; Subroutine: Compare A with R3
        ; Sets F0 = 1 if A < R3 (signed), F0 = 0 if A > R3
        XRL     A, R3             ; XOR to find sign difference
        ANL     A, #80H           ; Mask sign bit
        JZ      EQUAL_S           ; Both same sign bit
        ; Signs differ
        MOV     A, R3
        ANL     A, #80H
        JZ      A_NEG_R3          ; R3 positive, A negative → A < R3
        CLR     F0
        SJMP    DONE_S
A_NEG_R3: SETB   F0
        SJMP    DONE_S
EQUAL_S: ; Same sign — do unsigned compare
        MOV     A, R3
        CJNE    A, AR3P, NEQ
        CLR     F0
        SJMP    DONE_S
NEQ:    JC      R3_LESS
        CLR     F0
        SJMP    DONE_S
R3_LESS: SETB   F0
DONE_S: RET

AR3P:   EQU     0FBH              ; dummy label for syntax; actual code uses different structure
        END
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above assembly is illustrative. In practice, **Keil C51 with the `signed char` type** and the standard `>` operator handles signed comparison correctly because the compiler emits a signed-comparison code sequence automatically. **For exam purposes**, students are expected to *explain* the sign-bit handling conceptually.

**Sign-bit Discussion (7 marks total, 3 for code + 4 for explanation):**
- Bit 7 of an 8051 byte: 0 = positive, 1 = negative (2's-complement).
- `CJNE` uses **unsigned subtraction** internally — *cannot* directly compare signed values correctly.
- A proper signed compare first checks the sign bits: if they differ, the one with sign bit = 1 is smaller. If they are the same, the unsigned compare is valid.
- This is the foundation of all real-world signed-data processing in 8-bit embedded systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to seed with `A[0]`** — using `0x00` or `0xFF` instead → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Using wrong addressing mode** — using `@R0` on a code-memory array (needs `MOVC` with `@A+DPTR`, not `MOVX`/`MOV`) → **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Off-by-one in loop counter** — initializing `R2` with `N-1` instead of `N` (causes one element to be skipped) → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Not showing cycle count or time calculation** — KTU examiners specifically look for this in 14-mark assembly questions → **−2 to 3 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing `MOVX` and `MOVC`** — `MOVX` is for **external data memory**; `MOVC` is for **code (program) memory** where arrays are often stored in lab experiments. Using the wrong one will not work in Keil simulation → **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Failing to halt the program** with `SJMP HERE` or `while(1)` — the program "falls through" into unused memory and crashes → **−1 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Algorithm Type:** Single-pass linear scan — $O(N)$ time, $O(1)$ extra space.
- **Seeding Rule:** **Always** initialize `MAX` and `MIN` with `A[0]`. Never use `0x00` or `0xFF` as sentinels.
- **Comparisons Required:** Exactly $2(N-1)$ — one for MAX, one for MIN per element.
- **8051 Instructions to Master:** `CJNE` (compare), `JC` / `JNC` (conditional jump), `DJNZ` (loop control), `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` (code-memory read), `INC R0` (pointer increment).
- **Register Allocation:** `R0` = index pointer, `R2` = loop counter, `R3` = MAX holder, `R4` = MIN holder.
- **Memory Location for Arrays:** Internal RAM $\ge$ `0x30H` (above bit-addressable area to avoid clobbering).
- **Result Storage:** Typically `0x40H` and `0x41H` (or as specified in the question).
- **Crystal Frequency:** 8051 standard is 12 MHz → 1 machine cycle = $1\,\mu s$.
- **Signed vs Unsigned:** `CJNE` is **unsigned**; for signed data, special sign-bit handling is required (or use Keil C with `signed char`).
- **Output Verification:** Use **Keil µVision Debugger** → Watch Window for `R3`, `R4` and Memory Window for RAM locations.
- **Sorting Alternative:** Sorting to find extrema is $O(N^2)$ — wasteful. Always prefer single-pass scan.
- **Edge Cases Handled:** Empty array (undefined behavior in C; in assembly, ensure $N \ge 1$), single-element array (MAX = MIN = A[0]), all-equal array (no updates after seed).
- **Flowchart Mastery:** Be ready to draw the algorithm flowchart in exams — it carries 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Square and Cube of a Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Square and Cube of a Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PCBRL408 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems Lab**, the experiment on *"Square and Cube of a Number"* is categorized under the **8051 Assembly Language Programming** cluster of Module 2. Formally, it is defined as:

> A foundational arithmetic experiment in which a single-byte (8-bit) unsigned integer `N` (where `0 ≤ N ≤ 15` for safe square and `0 ≤ N ≤ 6` for safe cube on an 8-bit accumulator) is loaded into the **Accumulator (A)** of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller**, and the microcontroller is programmed to compute the mathematical operations `N²` and `N³` using the **MUL AB** instruction and a sequence of iterative `ADD`/`MUL` operations, with the final results stored in either internal **RAM (Data Memory)** or displayed via **Port P1/P2** to LEDs.

The mathematical operations themselves are defined as:

$$
\text{Square}(N) = N \times N = N^2
$$

$$
\text{Cube}(N) = N \times N \times N = N^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO):** This experiment directly maps to **CO2 – "Write 8051 assembly language programs for arithmetic, logical, and data transfer operations."** It is a **mandatory viva question** topic under Module 2 of the internal lab evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **shopkeeper with only one mental register (the Accumulator)**. You cannot multiply two big numbers at once — you only have a small scratchpad. So to find the **square** of `5`, you would:

1. Write `5` on the pad.
2. Add `5` to itself **5 times** → `5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 25`.
3. For the **cube**, you take that `25` and add it to itself **5 more times** → `125`.

This is exactly how an 8-bit 8051 behaves — it uses **repeated addition (`ADD A, R0`)** or the dedicated `MUL AB` instruction to emulate multiplication. There is **no native division or floating-point unit** in the standard 8051; arithmetic is built from primitive operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The 8051's Accumulator is **8 bits wide**, meaning it can only natively represent values from `0` to `255` (unsigned). Therefore, `N³` will **overflow** the accumulator for `N > 6`, since `7³ = 343 > 255`. This overflow behavior is itself a frequently tested viva question in KTU lab evaluations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Boundary Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| Accumulator Width | **8 bits** |
| Unsigned Max Value in A | **255** (0xFF) |
| Safe Square Range (0–255) | **N = 0 to 15** (since 15² = 225 ≤ 255) |
| Safe Cube Range (0–255) | **N = 0 to 6** (since 6³ = 216 ≤ 255) |
| Standard Test Input (KTU typical) | **N = 5** (Square = 25, Cube = 125) |
| Clock Frequency (typical) | **11.0592 MHz** (1 µs machine cycle) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown – The "How" and "Why"

The 8051 microcontroller does **not** have a dedicated "square" or "cube" instruction. Therefore, the experiment relies on building these operations from lower-level primitives. There are two pedagogical approaches taught in the KTU 2024 PCBRL408 syllabus:

### Approach A: Using the Hardware `MUL AB` Instruction
The 8051 contains a single hardware multiplier that performs unsigned 8-bit × 8-bit multiplication, producing a **16-bit result** stored across two registers:
- **High byte → B register**
- **Low byte → Accumulator (A)**

This is the **fastest** method (executes in **4 clock cycles ≈ 4 µs** at 11.0592 MHz).

### Approach B: Repeated Addition (Loop-Based Multiplication)
This is the **software emulated** method, preferred in early KTU lab cycles to demonstrate loop control with `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero). It teaches:
- Use of internal **RAM registers R0–R7** as counters.
- Implementation of a **nested loop structure** for the cube.
- Direct manipulation of the **Program Counter (PC)** via conditional jumps (`JNZ`, `DJNZ`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trend (2024 Scheme):** Most model answers in the lab record expect **both approaches** — the `MUL AB` approach for the square, and the repeated-addition approach for the cube, to demonstrate mastery of both hardware and software techniques.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

### For Square (N²):
1. **Load** the number `N` into a register (e.g., `R0`) and also into `A`.
2. **Multiply** `A` and `B` using `MUL AB`. The result (16-bit) appears in `B:A`.
3. **Store** the low byte (the square) in another register (e.g., `R1`) for display.

### For Cube (N³):
1. **Compute** the square first (stored in `R1`).
2. **Load** the square value back into `A`.
3. **Load** original `N` into `B`.
4. **Execute** `MUL AB` again.
5. The 16-bit result in `B:A` is the cube. Store the low byte in `R2`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we must check overflow:** Since the result of `MUL AB` is 16-bit, the high byte `B` is non-zero whenever the 8-bit result exceeds 255. For `N ≤ 15` (square) and `N ≤ 6` (cube), the high byte `B` remains zero, confirming no overflow occurred within the 8-bit boundary.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table uses `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) **inside math expressions** and standard pipes **only as column separators** in the markdown table syntax to comply with the engine's anti-break protocol.

| Operation | Mathematical Form | 8051 Instruction Sequence | Result Register | Execution Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Load operand | $A \leftarrow N$ | `MOV A, R0` | A | 1 µs |
| Prepare multiplier | $B \leftarrow N$ | `MOV B, R0` | B | 1 µs |
| Hardware Multiply | $(B:A) \leftarrow A \times B$ | `MUL AB` | B (high) : A (low) | 4 µs |
| Store square | $R1 \leftarrow A$ | `MOV R1, A` | R1 | 1 µs |
| Reload for cube | $A \leftarrow R1$ | `MOV A, R1` | A | 1 µs |
| Reload multiplier | $B \leftarrow R0$ | `MOV B, R0` | B | 1 µs |
| Second multiply | $(B:A) \leftarrow A \times B$ | `MUL AB` | B (high) : A (low) | 4 µs |
| Store cube | $R2 \leftarrow A$ | `MOV R2, A` | R2 | 1 µs |

**Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
- Max unsigned 8-bit value: $2^8 - 1 = 255$
- Max square within 8 bits: $\lfloor\sqrt{255}\rfloor = 15$ → $15^2 = 225$
- Max cube within 8 bits: $\lfloor\sqrt[3]{255}\rfloor = 6$ → $6^3 = 216$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The square and cube operations underpin critical embedded applications:

- **Cube Root Calculations** in **sensor calibration** (e.g., converting raw accelerometer data in g's to m/s² using $a = g\sqrt[3]{...}$).
- **Look-Up Table (LUT) Generation** for **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** algorithms such as the **CORDIC** algorithm used in GPS receivers.
- **Power Computation** in **battery management systems** ($P = V^2 / R$ for resistive loads).
- **Volume Computation** in **liquid level sensors** (cubic tank shapes).
- **Distance Squaring** in **Pythagorean computations** for **robotic localization** ($d^2 = x^2 + y^2$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Multiplication Algorithm

Let the input number be represented symbolically as $N$, where $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ and $0 \le N \le 9$ (typical BCD single digit). The objective is to derive both $N^2$ and $N^3$ in 8051 assembly.

### Derivation 3.1.1: The Square ($N^2$)

By the definition of multiplication as iterated addition:

$$
N^2 = N \times N = \underbrace{N + N + N + \cdots + N}_{N \text{ times}}
$$

The number of addition operations required is exactly $N$ when one operand is decremented to zero. This leads directly to the 8051 loop:

$$
\text{Result} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} N
$$

In assembly pseudocode, this becomes a single `DJNZ` loop:
```
R0 ← N          ; Counter
R1 ← 0          ; Accumulator for result
LOOP: ADD A, R1  ; A ← A + R1  (here R1 holds N)
      DJNZ R0, LOOP
```

### Derivation 3.1.2: The Cube ($N^3$)

The cube is mathematically defined as:

$$
N^3 = N \times N \times N
$$

We decompose this into two cascaded multiplications:

$$
N^3 = (N^2) \times N
$$

This means we first compute $N^2$ (using the loop above), then multiply that result by $N$ a second time. In 8051, this is achieved by a **nested loop structure**.

### Derivation 3.1.3: Hardware Multiply Shortcut

Using the 8051's built-in `MUL AB` instruction, we bypass the loop entirely:

$$
(A, B) \leftarrow A \cdot B
$$

The result of multiplication is stored as a 16-bit unsigned value:

$$
\text{HighByte} = B = \left\lfloor \frac{A \cdot B}{256} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
\text{LowByte} = A = (A \cdot B) \bmod 256
$$

For example, if $N = 5$:

$$
5 \times 5 = 25
$$

$$
\text{HighByte} = \left\lfloor \frac{25}{256} \right\rfloor = 0
$$

$$
\text{LowByte} = 25 \bmod 256 = 25
$$

And for the cube:

$$
5^3 = 125
$$

$$
\text{HighByte} = \left\lfloor \frac{125}{256} \right\rfloor = 0
$$

$$
\text{LowByte} = 125 \bmod 256 = 125
$$

Since both results are below 256, the high byte remains zero — confirming **no overflow**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Assembly Language Implementation (KEIL µVision Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a **complete, runnable 8051 assembly program** as expected in a KTU lab record. It is **not** Python — it is the native 8051 instruction set, which is the correct tooling for PCBRL408.

```asm
;==============================================================
; Program : Square and Cube of a Number (N = 5)
; Course  : PCBRL408 - Microcontrollers & Embedded Systems Lab
; Module  : 2 - 8051 Assembly Programming
; Tool    : KEIL uVision / EDSIM51 Simulator
;==============================================================

        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector address
START:  MOV     A, #05H         ; Load N = 5 into Accumulator
        MOV     R0, A           ; Save N in R0 (counter)
        MOV     R1, A           ; Save N in R1 (multiplier)

; ---------- COMPUTE SQUARE (N x N) ----------
SQUARE: MOV     B, R0           ; B = N
        MUL     AB              ; A = N x N (low byte), B = high byte
        MOV     R2, A           ; R2 stores the SQUARE result
        ; If we need to check overflow: JNB B, NO_OVF_SQ

; ---------- COMPUTE CUBE (N^2 x N) ----------
CUBE:   MOV     A, R2           ; A = N^2 (square result)
        MOV     B, R0           ; B = N
        MUL     AB              ; A = N^2 x N = N^3 (low byte)
        MOV     R3, A           ; R3 stores the CUBE result

; ---------- DISPLAY RESULTS ON PORT 1 ----------
DISPLAY_SQ:
        MOV     A, R2           ; Load square into A
        MOV     P1, A           ; Send to Port 1 (visible on LEDs)
        ACALL   DELAY           ; Small delay for visibility

DISPLAY_CB:
        MOV     A, R3           ; Load cube into A
        MOV     P2, A           ; Send to Port 2
        ACALL   DELAY

        SJMP    START           ; Infinite loop / restart

; ---------- DELAY SUBROUTINE ----------
DELAY:  MOV     R4, #0FFH
D1:     MOV     R5, #0FFH
D2:     DJNZ    R5, D2
        DJNZ    R4, D1
        RET

        END                     ; End of program
```

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Execution Trace (for N = 5)

| Step | Instruction | A (Hex) | B (Hex) | R0 | R1 | R2 | R3 | P1 | P2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `MOV A, #05H` | 05 | 00 | – | – | – | – | – | – |
| 2 | `MOV R0, A` | 05 | 00 | 05 | – | – | – | – | – |
| 3 | `MOV R1, A` | 05 | 00 | 05 | 05 | – | – | – | – |
| 4 | `MOV B, R0` | 05 | 05 | 05 | 05 | – | – | – | – |
| 5 | `MUL AB` | 19 | 00 | 05 | 05 | – | – | – | – |
| 6 | `MOV R2, A` | 19 | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | – | – | – |
| 7 | `MOV A, R2` | 19 | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | – | – | – |
| 8 | `MOV B, R0` | 19 | 05 | 05 | 05 | 19 | – | – | – |
| 9 | `MUL AB` | 7D | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | – | – | – |
| 10 | `MOV R3, A` | 7D | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | 7D | – | – |
| 11 | `MOV P1, A` (square) | 19 | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | 7D | 19H | – |
| 12 | `MOV P2, A` (cube) | 7D | 00 | 05 | 05 | 19 | 7D | 19H | 7DH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification in Decimal:**
> - Square result in R2: $0x19 = 1 \times 16 + 9 = 25 = 5^2$ ✓
> - Cube result in R3: $0x7D = 7 \times 16 + 13 = 125 = 5^3$ ✓
