Therefore, `RS1 = 1` and `RS0 = 1`.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{Bank}_\text{active} = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0} = 2 \cdot 1 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the starting RAM address.**

Register Bank 3 occupies internal RAM addresses `18H` to `1FH`.

**Step 5 — Map the registers.**

| Register | Address |
| :--- | :--- |
| R0 | `18H` |
| R1 | `19H` |
| R2 | `1AH` |
| R3 | `1BH` |
| R4 | `1CH` |
| R5 | `1DH` |
| R6 | `1EH` |
| R7 | `1FH` |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Program to Demonstrate ALL Five Addressing Modes

**AIM (KTU Standard Lab Record Format):** Write an 8051 ALP to transfer data between memory locations using all five addressing modes and verify the contents at each step.

### Complete Assembly Program

```assembly
ORG 0000H              ; Program starts at code address 0000H
START:
    ; ---- (1) IMMEDIATE ADDRESSING MODE ----
    MOV A, #25H        ; Load accumulator with immediate value 25H

    ; ---- (2) REGISTER ADDRESSING MODE ----
    MOV R0, A          ; Copy accumulator into register R0

    ; ---- (3) DIRECT ADDRESSING MODE ----
    MOV 30H, A         ; Copy accumulator to internal RAM address 30H

    ; ---- (4) REGISTER INDIRECT ADDRESSING MODE ----
    MOV R1, #40H       ; R1 acts as pointer to RAM location 40H
    MOV @R1, A         ; Copy A to address 40H indirectly

    ; ---- (5) INDEXED ADDRESSING MODE ----
    MOV DPTR, #2000H   ; DPTR points to base of look-up table in ROM
    MOV A, #02H        ; Offset = 02H
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR    ; Read ROM[2002H] into A

    ; ---- HALT / LOOP FOREVER ----
END:   SJMP END        ; Stay in infinite loop so we can observe registers

    ; ---- LOOK-UP TABLE (placed in code ROM) ----
    ORG 2000H
TABLE: DB 11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H   ; 5-byte LUT
       END
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 — Program Memory Map.**

| Address (ROM) | Content | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `0000H` | `74 25` | Opcode `MOV A, #25H` |
| `0002H` | `F8` | Opcode `MOV R0, A` |
| `0003H` | `F5 30` | Opcode `MOV 30H, A` |
| `0005H` | `79 40` | Opcode `MOV R1, #40H` |
| `0007H` | `F7` | Opcode `MOV @R1, A` |
| `0008H` | `90 20 00` | Opcode `MOV DPTR, #2000H` |
| `000BH` | `74 02` | Opcode `MOV A, #02H` |
| `000DH` | `E4` | Opcode `CLR A` (prepared before MOVC) |
| `000EH` | `93` | Opcode `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |
| `000FH` | `80 FE` | Opcode `SJMP END` |
| `2000H` | `11 22 33 44 55` | Look-up table data |

**Step 2 — Internal RAM Map after execution.**

| Address (RAM) | Content (Hex) | Set by which instruction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `00H` (R0 of Bank 0) | `25H` | `MOV R0, A` (Register mode) |
| `30H` | `25H` | `MOV 30H, A` (Direct mode) |
| `40H` | `25H` | `MOV @R1, A` (Register Indirect) |
| `A` (Acc) | `33H` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (Indexed) |
| DPTR | `2000H` | `MOV DPTR, #2000H` |

**Step 3 — Verify the indexed fetch.**

$$
\text{EA} = 2000\text{H} + 02\text{H} = 2002\text{H}
$$

ROM at `2002H` = `33H`, therefore `A = 33H` after the MOVC.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Cycle Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most valuable KTU viva reference**. Examiners routinely ask: *"Which addressing mode generates the fewest machine cycles?"*

| Addressing Mode | Operand Fetches (Internal) | External Memory Cycle | Typical Machine Cycles (8051) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Immediate | 1 (code ROM) | No | **1** |
| Register | 0 (internal to CPU) | No | **1** |
| Direct | 1 (internal RAM) | No | **1** |
| Register Indirect (8-bit) | 1 (internal RAM) | No | **1** |
| Register Indirect (16-bit, MOVX) | 1 (external RAM) | **Yes** | **2** |
| Indexed (MOVC) | 1 (code ROM) | **Yes** | **2** |

> [!NOTE]
> Although Register Indirect and Direct modes both take **1 machine cycle**, the direct mode uses an **8-bit address embedded in the instruction** (larger opcode), while indirect mode uses a **1-byte opcode referencing a register pointer**. This makes indirect mode more code-efficient in loops.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 Data Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Counter PC] --> B[Fetch Opcode from Code ROM]
    B --> C{Decode Addressing Mode}
    C -->|Immediate| D[Fetch Data Byte from Next Code ROM Location]
    C -->|Register| E[Read Data from R0-R7 inside CPU]
    C -->|Direct| F[Read Data from Internal RAM address field]
    C -->|Register Indirect| G[Read Pointer from R0 or R1]
    G --> H[Use Pointer as Address into Internal or External RAM]
    C -->|Indexed| I[Compute EA = A + DPTR]
    I --> J[Read Data from Code ROM at EA]
    D --> K[Write Data to Destination A or RAM]
    E --> K
    F --> K
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Execute Next Instruction]
```

## 4.2 Memory Map Showing Each Addressing Mode's Reach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CodeROM["Code ROM 64KB 0000H to FFFFH"]
        LUT[Look-up Table at 2000H - accessed by Indexed mode]
        PROG[Program Instructions - accessed by Immediate mode]
    end
    subgraph ExtRAM["External Data RAM 64KB 0000H to FFFFH"]
        EXTRAM[Accessed by MOVX with DPTR - Register Indirect 16-bit]
    end
    subgraph IntRAM["Internal RAM 256 bytes 00H to FFH"]
        subgraph Lower128["Lower 128 bytes 00H to 7FH"]
            RB0[Register Bank 0 00H to 07H - Register mode]
            RB1[Register Bank 1 08H to 0FH]
            RB2[Register Bank 2 10H to 17H]
            RB3[Register Bank 3 18H to 1FH]
            BITAR[Bit Addressable 20H to 2FH]
            GEN[General Purpose 30H to 7FH - accessed by Direct and Register Indirect]
        end
        subgraph Upper128["Upper 128 bytes 80H to FFH - SFRs only"]
            SFR[Special Function Registers 80H to FFH - accessed by Direct mode]
        end
    end
    PROG -->|Immediate| CPU[CPU Core via Accumulator A]
    LUT -->|Indexed| CPU
    EXTRAM -->|Register Indirect 16-bit| CPU
    RB0 -->|Register mode| CPU
    GEN -->|Direct and Register Indirect| CPU
    SFR -->|Direct mode| CPU
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Correct Addressing Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to transfer data in 8051] --> Q1{Is the data value known at assembly time?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, constant| IM[Use Immediate mode MOV A, #data]
    Q1 -->|No, runtime value| Q2{Is the data in R0 to R7?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| REG[Use Register mode MOV A, Rn]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the data at a fixed RAM or SFR address?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| DIR[Use Direct mode MOV A, address]
    Q3 -->|No, address computed at runtime| Q4{Need to access code ROM LUT?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| IDX[Use Indexed mode MOVC A, @A+DPTR]
    Q4 -->|No, need pointer-based RAM access| IND[Use Register Indirect mode MOV A, @Ri]
```

## 4.4 Execution Sequence Diagram for Worked Example 3

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant ROM as Code ROM
    participant RAM as Internal RAM
    participant SFR as SFR Region

    Note over CPU: PC = 0000H at start

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode 74H at 0000H
    ROM-->>CPU: Opcode MOV A, #data
    CPU->>ROM: Fetch 25H at 0001H
    ROM-->>CPU: Data = 25H
    Note over CPU: A = 25H  [IMMEDIATE MODE COMPLETE]

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode F8H at 0002H
    Note over CPU: R0 = A = 25H  [REGISTER MODE COMPLETE]

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode F5H at 0003H
    CPU->>ROM: Fetch address 30H at 0004H
    CPU->>RAM: Write 25H to address 30H
    Note over RAM: RAM[30H] = 25H  [DIRECT MODE COMPLETE]

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode 79H at 0005H
    CPU->>ROM: Fetch 40H at 0006H
    Note over CPU: R1 = 40H  [IMMEDIATE used for pointer]

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode F7H at 0007H
    CPU->>RAM: Write A to address pointed by R1
    Note over RAM: RAM[40H] = 25H  [REGISTER INDIRECT COMPLETE]

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode 90H at 0008H
    CPU->>ROM: Fetch 2000H at 0009H and 000AH
    Note over CPU: DPTR = 2000H

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch 74H at 000BH
    CPU->>ROM: Fetch 02H at 000CH
    Note over CPU: A = 02H

    CPU->>ROM: Fetch opcode 93H at 000DH
    CPU->>ROM: Read byte at EA = 2002H
    ROM-->>CPU: Returns 33H
    Note over CPU: A = 33H  [INDEXED MODE COMPLETE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "addressing mode" in the context of the 8051 microcontroller. List all five addressing modes supported by the 8051 with one example instruction for each.**

**Model Answer (for 3-mark valuation):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An addressing mode is the method by which the 8051 specifies the operand (data or address) used by an instruction. It tells the CPU *where* to find the data.

> **Five modes with examples (2 marks):**
> 1. **Immediate** — `MOV A, #25H`
> 2. **Register** — `MOV A, R3`
> 3. **Direct** — `MOV A, 30H`
> 4. **Register Indirect** — `MOV A, @R0`
> 5. **Indexed** — `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Direct Addressing Mode and Register Indirect Addressing Mode in 8051. Mention one application where Register Indirect mode is preferred over Direct mode.**

**Model Answer (for 3-mark valuation):**

> | Feature | Direct Mode | Register Indirect Mode |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Address source | 8-bit field embedded in instruction | Contents of R0 or R1 |
> | Instruction size | 2 bytes | 1 byte |
> | Use case | Accessing fixed RAM/SFR locations | Loop-based array/traversal access |
> | Range | Internal RAM only (00H–FFH) | Can be extended to external RAM via MOVX |
>
> **Application (1 mark):** Iterating through an array stored in internal RAM by incrementing the pointer register inside a loop — this is impossible with Direct mode because the address would have to be hard-coded for every access.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> - Students **lose 2 marks** if they confuse the symbol `#` (immediate) with the symbol `@` (indirect) in their answer sheets. The hash means the **data is right there**; the at-symbol means **go look at the address stored in the register**.
> - In Indexed Addressing, students often forget that **`MOVC` fetches from Code ROM, not data RAM**. Using `MOVX` instead in a code-memory access loses **3 marks** outright.
> - Always **explicitly state the byte size** of the instruction when discussing addressing modes. Examiners reward this with 1 extra mark.
> - For Part B questions, **trace the program execution line by line** rather than writing a vague summary. The valuation key allocates 1 mark per correctly traced line for code segments up to 6 lines.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a)** Explain the **Indexed Addressing Mode** of the 8051 with a neat diagram. Derive the effective address when `DPTR = 4500H` and `A = 10H`. If the code ROM at that effective address contains the value `A5H`, what will be the contents of the accumulator after executing `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`? **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Write an 8051 ALP to copy a block of **10 bytes** of data from internal RAM locations starting at `30H` to locations starting at `50H` using **Register Indirect Addressing Mode**. Draw the program trace and show the final memory map. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
Indexed Addressing Mode is used to access **code ROM** (program memory). The effective address is computed by adding the **8-bit accumulator content** to a **16-bit base register (DPTR or PC)**. The mnemonic used is `MOVC` (Move Code).

**Diagram / Flow (1 mark):**
Refer to Section 4.3 — Indexed mode is selected when the programmer needs to access a Look-Up Table (LUT) stored in code ROM.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad \text{DPTR} &= 4500\text{H} \\
\text{A} &= 10\text{H} \\[4pt]
\text{Effective Address} \; \text{EA} &= (\text{A}) + (\text{DPTR}) \\[4pt]
\text{EA} &= 0010\text{H} + 4500\text{H} \\[4pt]
\text{EA} &= 4510\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (1 mark):** The CPU reads code ROM at `4510H`, finds `A5H`, and loads it into the accumulator. **Final A = A5H**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]

**Algorithm (2 marks):**
Use R0 as a source pointer and R1 as a destination pointer. Initialize both, then loop 10 times, copying one byte per iteration and incrementing both pointers.

**Program (4 marks):**

```assembly
ORG 0000H
    MOV R0, #30H        ; Source pointer = 30H
    MOV R1, #50H        ; Destination pointer = 50H
    MOV R2, #0AH        ; Counter = 10
LOOP:
    MOV A, @R0          ; Read source byte [REGISTER INDIRECT]
    MOV @R1, A          ; Write to destination [REGISTER INDIRECT]
    INC R0              ; Source pointer++
    INC R1              ; Destination pointer++
    DJNZ R2, LOOP       ; Decrement counter, jump if not zero
END: SJMP END
    END
```

**Final Memory Map (1 mark):**

| Destination | Content (after execution) |
| :--- | :--- |
| `50H` | Old value of `30H` |
| `51H` | Old value of `31H` |
| ... | ... |
| `59H` | Old value of `39H` |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Compare the **Immediate, Direct, and Register Indirect** addressing modes in terms of (i) instruction size, (ii) machine cycles, (iii) operand location, and (iv) one typical use case. Present the answer in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Write and explain an 8051 assembly program that demonstrates data transfer using **all five addressing modes** by moving a constant value `55H` through every mode. Show the contents of the accumulator, registers R0–R7, internal RAM at `30H`, and the final indexed result. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute as 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2.5):**

| Parameter | Immediate | Direct | Register Indirect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Instruction size | 2 or 3 bytes | 2 bytes | 1 byte |
| (ii) Machine cycles | 1 cycle | 1 cycle | 1 cycle (internal) / 2 cycles (external via MOVX) |
| (iii) Operand location | Embedded in instruction (code ROM) | Internal RAM or SFR address (00H–FFH) | RAM location pointed by R0 or R1 |
| (iv) Typical use | Loading constants like `MOV A, #00H` | Accessing SFRs like `MOV A, P1` | Array traversal, buffer copying |

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Program (4 marks):**

```assembly
ORG 0000H
    ; IMMEDIATE
    MOV A, #55H         ; A = 55H

    ; REGISTER
    MOV R0, A           ; R0 = 55H

    ; DIRECT
    MOV 30H, A          ; RAM[30H] = 55H

    ; REGISTER INDIRECT
    MOV R1, #40H        ; Pointer setup
    MOV @R1, A          ; RAM[40H] = 55H

    ; INDEXED
    MOV DPTR, #3000H    ; LUT base address
    MOV A, #03H         ; Offset
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; A = ROM[3003H]
    END_LOOP: SJMP END_LOOP

    ORG 3000H
    LUT: DB 10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H
    END
```

**Trace Table (2 marks):**

| Stage | A | R0 | R1 | RAM[30H] | RAM[40H] | Effective Address |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| After Immediate | 55H | – | – | – | – | – |
| After Register | 55H | 55H | – | – | – | – |
| After Direct | 55H | 55H | – | 55H | – | – |
| After Reg Indirect | 55H | 55H | 40H | 55H | 55H | – |
| After Indexed | 40H | 55H | 40H | 55H | 55H | 3003H |

**Final Result (1 mark):** The accumulator holds `40H` after the indexed fetch because `EA = 3000H + 03H = 3003H` and the LUT stores `40H` at offset 3.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your lab record!)**

- ⭐ The 8051 supports **exactly 5 addressing modes** for data transfer: **Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed**.
- ⭐ **`MOV`** is the universal data-transfer instruction. **`MOVC`** is for code ROM (Indexed). **`MOVX`** is for external RAM (16-bit Register Indirect).
- ⭐ The **`#` symbol** means "the value is right here" (Immediate). The **`@` symbol** means "look at what the register points to" (Indirect).
- ⭐ The **Indexed mode effective address** is always computed as:
  $$\text{EA} = (\text{A}) + (\text{DPTR}) \quad \text{or} \quad (\text{A}) + (\text{PC})$$
- ⭐ **Register Addressing Mode** is the **fastest** (1 byte, 1 cycle, no memory fetch).
- ⭐ **Direct Mode** can access the **entire lower 128 bytes of internal RAM plus the SFR region** (addresses 00H–FFH).
- ⭐ **Register Indirect Mode** is mandatory for **loop-based memory access** because the address is not hard-coded.
- ⭐ **Register Bank selection** is governed by `RS1:RS0` bits of PSW: `00 → Bank 0`, `01 → Bank 1`, `10 → Bank 2`, `11 → Bank 3`.
- ⭐ **Destination can NEVER be Immediate** — only the source operand can use `#`.
- ⭐ The **`XCH` and `XCHD`** instructions use Register Addressing Mode to swap accumulator content with a register.
- ⭐ For **lab records**, always include: **Aim, Algorithm, Program, Output Memory Map, Result** — the KTU valuation key explicitly rewards this structure with 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Square Wave Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Square Wave Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Square Wave Generation** using a microcontroller is the process of producing a periodic digital waveform that alternates symmetrically (typically at 50% duty cycle) between logic HIGH and logic LOW states on a specific port pin, where the timing is precisely controlled by the on-chip hardware **Timers/Counters** of the microcontroller.

In the context of KTU's **PCBRL408 (8051 Architecture-based) Lab**, square wave generation is implemented using the **Intel 8051 family's programmable timers (Timer 0 and Timer 1)** running in **Mode 1 (16-bit timer mode)** or **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload mode)**, with a port pin (commonly P1.0) toggled in the **Timer Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Square wave generation is listed as a **mandatory timer-based experiment** in Module 2 of PCBRL408, tested as a **Part B (14-mark) lab viva + written program** question in the Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-world Analogy — The Traffic Signal Controller:**

Imagine a traffic policeman standing at an intersection who flips a sign ("GO" / "STOP") at exact, regular intervals — say every 500 milliseconds. He does NOT keep looking at his wristwatch; instead, he relies on a **precise mechanical timer** that "dings" every half-second, and each time it dings, he flips the sign.

The **8051 microcontroller** behaves identically:
- The **on-chip Timer** acts as the "mechanical timer" — it counts internal machine cycles.
- When the timer **overflows** (ding!), it triggers an **interrupt**.
- The **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is the "traffic policeman" — it simply **toggles** the output pin.
- The result on the output pin is a **square wave**, whose half-period equals the timer's overflow delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The square wave's frequency is entirely determined by the **Crystal Oscillator frequency** and the **Timer reload value**. A 1 kHz square wave requires a full period of 1 ms, meaning the pin must be toggled every **500 µs** (half-period).

### 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Standard KTU Value |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Crystal Frequency ($f_{osc}$)** | **11.0592 MHz** (most common) or 12 MHz |
| **Machine Cycle Period ($T_{mc}$)** | **12 / $f_{osc}$** = 1.085 µs (for 11.0592 MHz) |
| **Timer Resolution** | 1 Machine Cycle per count |
| **Typical Output Pin** | **P1.0** (Port 1, Bit 0) |
| **Standard Duty Cycle** | **50%** (symmetric) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Crystal Oscillator Drives the System**
The external quartz crystal connected to pins **XTAL1 and XTAL2** of the 8051 generates the master clock. For a standard 8051 development board used in KTU labs, this is **11.0592 MHz** (chosen specifically because it divides evenly for standard baud rates).

**Step 2: Machine Cycle Generation**
The 8051 internally divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to produce the machine cycle, which is the basic timing unit for all instructions and timer increments.

$$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}, \quad T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 3: Timer Configuration**
The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is configured to select:
- **Timer 0 or Timer 1** (M0–M1 bits select mode, C/T̄ bit selects counter/timer)
- **Mode 1 (16-bit)** — Timer counts from the loaded value up to **65535**, then overflows to 0 on the next count, raising the timer overflow flag (TF0 or TF1).
- **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** — Timer counts from the loaded value up to **255**, then automatically reloads the initial value and continues (useful for precise, repeatable square waves).

**Step 4: Timer Loading**
For a desired time delay, the initial 16-bit value is calculated and split between **TH0 (high byte)** and **TL0 (low byte)**.

**Step 5: Interrupt Enable and Timer Start**
- `SETB ET0` — Enable Timer 0 interrupt
- `SETB EA` — Enable Global Interrupt
- `SETB TR0` — Start Timer 0 counting

**Step 6: ISR Execution on Overflow**
When the timer overflows, the 8051 automatically:
1. Sets the **TF0 flag**
2. Generates an **interrupt request** to the CPU
3. The CPU pushes the current PC onto the stack
4. Control transfers to the **ISR at address 000BH** (for Timer 0)
5. The ISR **toggles the output pin** (CPL P1.0) and **reloads** the timer
6. The `RETI` instruction returns control to the main program

**Step 7: Continuous Square Wave Output**
The pin toggling in the ISR produces a continuous square wave whose half-period equals the timer delay.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
|---------|------------|----------|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | Convert oscillator freq to timer tick duration |
| Timer Count Value | $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}}$ | Number of timer increments for a desired delay |
| Timer Reload (16-bit) | $\text{TH:T L} = 65536 - N$ | Hex value to load into TH and TL registers |
| TH0 Calculation | $\text{TH0} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{65536 - N}{256} \right\rfloor$ | High byte of timer reload |
| TL0 Calculation | $\text{TL0} = (65536 - N) \mod 256$ | Low byte of timer reload |
| Square Wave Half-Period | $T_{half} = T_{delay}$ | Time between two consecutive pin toggles |
| Square Wave Full Period | $T = 2 \times T_{half}$ | Time for one complete HIGH+LOW cycle |
| Square Wave Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{delay}}$ | Output frequency in Hz |
| **Standard Crystal** | $f_{osc} = \mathbf{11.0592\ MHz}$ | Default KTU lab crystal |
| $T_{mc}$ for Standard | $T_{mc} = \mathbf{1.085\ \mu s}$ | Machine cycle at 11.0592 MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** For $N = 0$ (maximum delay), the timer rolls over after $65536 \times T_{mc}$. For $N = 1$ (minimum delay), the timer overflows after $T_{mc}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Square wave generation via microcontrollers is foundational in:
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** for motor speed control (DC motors, servo motors)
- **Clock generation** for digital systems and communication protocols (SPI, I2C)
- **Buzzer/Alarm generation** in embedded alarm systems
- **Function generators** — digital synthesis of arbitrary waveforms
- **Power electronics** — gate drive signals for MOSFETs and IGBTs in SMPS circuits

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of Timer Reload Value for a 1 kHz Square Wave

**Problem Statement:** Design an 8051-based square wave generator to produce a **1 kHz** square wave on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Assume crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$.

#### Step 1: Determine the Required Square Wave Period

The frequency is given as $f = 1\ \text{kHz}$, so the full period is:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000\ \text{Hz}} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1000\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Explanation:** The square wave must complete one HIGH + LOW cycle every 1 millisecond. This is the fundamental constraint of the design.

#### Step 2: Determine the Half-Period (Timer Delay)

Since the pin must be toggled once every half-period to generate a 50% duty cycle square wave, the timer must produce a delay equal to:

$$T_{delay} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1000\ \mu\text{s}}{2} = 500\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Explanation:** Each ISR entry toggles the pin, so the time between two toggles is the half-period. Two toggles = one full cycle.

#### Step 3: Calculate the Machine Cycle Period

With $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Explanation:** The 8051 divides the oscillator by 12 to get the machine cycle. Each timer tick advances the timer register by 1 every 1.085 µs.

#### Step 4: Calculate the Required Timer Count

The number of timer increments needed for the 500 µs delay:

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{500\ \mu\text{s}}{1.085\ \mu\text{s}} = 460.83$$

Since the timer must count whole cycles, we round down:

$$N = 460$$

**Explanation:** A delay of 460 machine cycles produces $460 \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s} = 499.1\ \mu\text{s}$, which is the closest achievable approximation.

#### Step 5: Calculate the 16-bit Timer Reload Value

For Mode 1 (16-bit timer, rolls over at 65536):

$$\text{Reload} = 65536 - N = 65536 - 460 = 65076$$

Convert 65076 to hexadecimal:

$$65076 \div 256 = 254\ \text{remainder}\ 52$$
$$254 = \text{FE}_{16}, \quad 52 = 34_{16}$$
$$\therefore \text{Reload} = \text{FE34}_{16}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{TH0} = \text{FEH}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{34H}$$

#### Step 6: Verify the Output Frequency

The actual delay produced:

$$T_{delay,actual} = 460 \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s} = 499.1\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{actual} = 2 \times 499.1\ \mu\text{s} = 998.2\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$f_{actual} = \frac{1}{998.2\ \mu\text{s}} = 1001.8\ \text{Hz}$$

**Error:** $\vert 1001.8 - 1000 \vert / 1000 \times 100\% = 0.18\%$ — well within acceptable tolerance for embedded applications.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Lab Square Wave Design Problems

| Target Frequency | Half-Period $T_{delay}$ | Count $N$ | Reload (Hex) | TH0 | TL0 |
|------------------|------------------------|-----------|--------------|-----|-----|
| 100 Hz | 5000 µs | 4608 | FE20H | FEH | 20H |
| 500 Hz | 1000 µs | 921 | FC67H | FCH | 67H |
| **1 kHz** | **500 µs** | **460** | **FE34H** | **FEH** | **34H** |
| 2 kHz | 250 µs | 230 | FF1AH | FFH | 1AH |
| 5 kHz | 100 µs | 92 | FFA4H | FFH | A4H |
| 10 kHz | 50 µs | 46 | FFD2H | FFH | D2H |
| 50 kHz | 10 µs | 9 | FFF7H | FFH | F7H |

### 3.3 Complete 8051 Assembly Program for 1 kHz Square Wave

```asm
ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN        ; Jump to main program at reset

ORG 000BH           ; Timer 0 Interrupt Vector Address
    CPL P1.0         ; Toggle P1.0 (complement the pin)
    MOV TH0, #0FEH   ; Reload high byte of Timer 0
    MOV TL0, #034H   ; Reload low byte of Timer 0
    RETI             ; Return from interrupt

ORG 0030H           ; Main program starts here
MAIN:
    MOV TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    MOV TH0, #0FEH   ; Initial high byte
    MOV TL0, #034H   ; Initial low byte
    SETB P1.0        ; Set P1.0 HIGH initially
    SETB ET0         ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    SETB EA          ; Enable global interrupt
    SETB TR0         ; Start Timer 0
HERE: SJMP HERE      ; Infinite idle loop
END
```

### 3.4 Equivalent Embedded C Program (Keil / SDCC compatible)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1 {
    WAVE = ~WAVE;      // Toggle P1.0
    TH0 = 0xFE;        // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x34;        // Reload low byte
}

void main(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;       // Timer 0, Mode 1
    TH0 = 0xFE;
    TL0 = 0x34;
    WAVE = 1;          // Initial state HIGH
    ET0 = 1;           // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA  = 1;           // Enable global interrupt
    TR0 = 1;           // Start Timer 0
    while(1);          // Idle forever
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hardware Block Diagram — 8051 Square Wave Generator Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CRYSTAL["Quartz Crystal 11.0592 MHz"] --> OSC["Oscillator Circuit"]
    OSC --> CPU["8051 CPU Core"]
    CPU --> TMR0["Timer 0 Module"]
    TMR0 --> REG["TMOD TH0 TL0 TCON"]
    REG --> ISR["Interrupt Controller"]
    ISR --> VEC["Vector Address 000BH"]
    VEC --> TOGGLE["Toggle P1.0 Logic"]
    TOGGLE --> PORT["Port 1 Latch"]
    PORT --> PIN["Pin P1.0 Output"]
    PIN --> SCOPE["CRO / Oscilloscope Display"]
    PIN --> LED["LED Indicator"]
    CRYSTAL2["Reset Circuit"] --> CPU
    EA_EN["EA and ET0 Flags"] --> ISR
```

### 4.2 Program Execution Flow — ISR-Driven Square Wave

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start Main Program"] --> initTMOD["Set TMOD equals 01H Mode 1"]
    initTMOD --> initTimer["Load TH0 FEH TL0 34H"]
    initTimer --> setPin["Set P1.0 HIGH"]
    setPin --> enInt["Enable ET0 and EA"]
    enInt --> startT0["Set TR0 to start Timer 0"]
    startT0 --> idleLoop["Main Loop SJMP HERE"]
    idleLoop -.waits.-> idleLoop
    startT0 -.overflow.-> isrEntry["ISR Entry Address 000BH"]
    isrEntry --> toggle["CPL P1.0 Toggle Pin"]
    toggle --> reload["Reload TH0 FEH TL0 34H"]
    reload --> reti["RETI Return from ISR"]
    reti --> idleLoop
```

### 4.3 Timer Operation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> COUNTING: TR0 Set
    COUNTING --> OVERFLOW: Timer reaches FFFFH
    OVERFLOW --> TOGGLE_PIN: Generate Interrupt
    TOGGLE_PIN --> RELOAD: Load new value into TH0 TL0
    RELOAD --> COUNTING: Auto restart via TR0
    OVERFLOW --> IDLE: TR0 Cleared
```

```mermaid
    wHigh1 --> wLow1["LOW 500 us"]
    wLow1 --> wHigh2["HIGH 500 us"]
    wHigh2 --> wLow2["LOW 500 us"]
    wLow2 --> wHigh3["HIGH 500 us"]
    wHigh3 --> tmark["T equals 1 ms equals 1 over 1kHz"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define square wave generation using 8051 microcontroller. Mention the role of TMOD and TCON registers in the process.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Square wave generation using 8051 is the process of producing a periodic digital waveform on an output port pin by **toggling the pin inside a Timer Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** at precisely calculated intervals.

- **TMOD Register:** Selects the operating mode of the timer. For Mode 1, `TMOD = 0x01` (Timer 0, 16-bit). The M0, M1 bits choose the mode, and the C/T̄ bit selects counter or timer operation.
- **TCON Register:** Controls the timer operation. The **TR0** bit starts/stops Timer 0, and the **TF0** bit is set when Timer 0 overflows (causing the interrupt).

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark TMOD + 1 mark TCON)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
List any **four** SFRs (Special Function Registers) used in 8051 square wave generation. State one function of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| SFR | Function |
|-----|----------|
| **TMOD** | Configures timer mode (Mode 0/1/2) and counter/timer selection |
| **TCON** | Controls timer run flag (TR0/TR1) and overflow flag (TF0/TF1) |
| **TH0** | Holds the high byte of Timer 0's 16-bit count value |
| **TL0** | Holds the low byte of Timer 0's 16-bit count value |
| **IE** | Interrupt Enable register; EA enables global, ET0 enables Timer 0 interrupt |
| **P1** | Port 1, used as output port for the generated square wave |

(Any four = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a **2 kHz square wave on P1.5** using **Timer 1 in Mode 1**. Assume crystal frequency = 11.0592 MHz. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the timer reload value for TH1 and TL1, and explain why Mode 1 requires manual reloading. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $f = 2\ \text{kHz}$, so $T = 500\ \mu\text{s}$, $T_{delay} = 250\ \mu\text{s}$

**Reload Calculation:**
$$N = \frac{250\ \mu\text{s}}{1.085\ \mu\text{s}} \approx 230$$
$$\text{Reload} = 65536 - 230 = 65306 = \text{FF1A}_{16}$$
$$\text{TH1} = \text{FFH}, \quad \text{TL1} = \text{1AH}$$

**Assembly Program:**

```asm
ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN

ORG 001BH            ; Timer 1 ISR vector
    CPL P1.5          ; Toggle P1.5
    MOV TH1, #0FFH    ; Reload high byte
    MOV TL1, #01AH    ; Reload low byte
    RETI

ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    MOV TMOD, #10H    ; Timer 1, Mode 1 (0001 0000)
    MOV TH1, #0FFH
    MOV TL1, #01AH
    SETB P1.5
    SETB ET1          ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt
    SETB EA
    SETB TR1          ; Start Timer 1
LOOP: SJMP LOOP
END
```

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Calculation shown above:** $N = 230$, reload = FF1A, TH1 = FFH, TL1 = 1AH.

**Why Mode 1 needs manual reloading:**

In **Mode 1 (16-bit timer mode)**, the timer is configured as a 16-bit register formed by **THx (8 bits) and TLx (8 bits)**. When the timer starts from the loaded value and counts up to **FFFFH + 1 = 10000H**, the overflow flag (TFx) is set, and the timer **rolls over to 0000H** — it does **NOT** automatically reload the initial value.

This means in the ISR, the programmer **must explicitly reload** both THx and TLx with the original value to ensure the next delay is of the same duration. Without manual reloading, the timer would start from 0000H on the next cycle, producing an entirely different (much longer) delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (—2 marks):** Forgetting to reload THx and TLx in the ISR. This causes the first cycle to be correct (e.g., 250 µs) but subsequent cycles to be at the maximum possible delay (~71 ms for 16-bit timer at 11.0592 MHz), destroying the square wave.

In contrast, **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** uses only TLx for counting and automatically reloads THx into TLx on overflow — no manual reload is needed in the ISR, making it preferable for high-frequency square waves.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **four modes of Timer 0** in 8051. Compare Mode 1 and Mode 2 for square wave generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an 8051 program to generate a **500 Hz square wave with 60% duty cycle** on pin P1.2. Show all timer and reload calculations. Crystal = 11.0592 MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Four Modes of Timer 0 (controlled by M1, M0 bits of TMOD):**

| Mode | M1 M0 | Description | Use |
|------|-------|-------------|-----|
| **Mode 0** | 0 0 | 13-bit timer (8048 legacy mode) | Rarely used |
| **Mode 1** | 0 1 | **16-bit timer** (TH0 + TL0) | Most flexible, manual reload in ISR |
| **Mode 2** | 1 0 | **8-bit auto-reload** (TL0 counts, TH0 holds reload value) | Preferred for square waves |
| **Mode 3** | 1 1 | Split timer (TL0 + TH0 as separate 8-bit timers) | Special applications |

**Comparison: Mode 1 vs Mode 2 for Square Wave Generation:**

| Feature | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) |
|---------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Resolution | 16-bit (65536 counts) | 8-bit (256 counts max) |
| Maximum delay | ~71 ms @ 11.0592 MHz | ~278 µs @ 11.0592 MHz |
| Reload | Manual in ISR (2 instructions) | Automatic by hardware |
| Frequency range | **Low to medium (Hz to low kHz)** | **Medium to high (kHz range)** |
| Accuracy | Very high (large count = small % error) | Lower (rounding error significant) |
| Programming complexity | Slightly more code | Simpler, cleaner code |
| **KTU recommendation** | For frequencies < 5 kHz | For frequencies > 5 kHz |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$, **Duty Cycle = 60%** (asymmetric square wave)

**Step 1: Calculate Full Period and Sub-Periods**

$$T = \frac{1}{500} = 2000\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{HIGH} = 0.60 \times 2000 = 1200\ \mu\text{s}$$
$$T_{LOW} = 0.40 \times 2000 = 800\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Timer Counts**

$$N_{HIGH} = \frac{1200}{1.085} \approx 1106$$
$$N_{LOW} = \frac{800}{1.085} \approx 737$$

**Step 3: Calculate Reload Values**

$$\text{Reload}_{HIGH} = 65536 - 1106 = 64430 = \text{FBA6}_{16}$$
$$\text{TH0}_{HIGH} = \text{FBH}, \quad \text{TL0}_{HIGH} = \text{A6H}$$

$$\text{Reload}_{LOW} = 65536 - 737 = 64799 = \text{FD1F}_{16}$$
$$\text{TH0}_{LOW} = \text{FDH}, \quad \text{TL0}_{LOW} = \text{1FH}$$

**Step 4: Assembly Program (Asymmetric Square Wave)**

```asm
ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN

ORG 000BH            ; Timer 0 ISR
    JB FLAG, HIGH_DONE  ; If FLAG=1, currently in HIGH phase
    ; --- Currently in LOW phase, transition to HIGH ---
    SETB FLAG
    SETB P1.2
    MOV TH0, #0FBH
    MOV TL0, #0A6H    ; 1200 us HIGH
    RETI
HIGH_DONE:
    ; --- HIGH phase done, transition to LOW ---
    CLR FLAG
    CLR P1.2
    MOV TH0, #0FDH
    MOV TL0, #01FH    ; 800 us LOW
    RETI

ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1
    MOV TH0, #0FBH
    MOV TL0, #0A6H
    CLR FLAG          ; Start with LOW phase logic
    SETB P1.2
    SETB ET0
    SETB EA
    SETB TR0
LOOP: SJMP LOOP
END
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in Square Wave Generation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $f_{osc}$ to Machine Cycle** — Many students directly divide the time delay by 12 MHz instead of first computing $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$. This gives an off-by-12× answer. **(—2 to —3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing the 16-bit reload formula** — Writing `TH0 = 65536 - N` instead of `TH0 = (65536 - N)/256` and `TL0 = (65536 - N) % 256`. The high and low bytes must be split correctly. **(—1 to —2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Missing manual reload in Mode 1 ISR** — Loading TH0/TL0 only in the main program and not in the ISR. This works for the first cycle only. **(—2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting `SETB EA`** — Without the global interrupt enable, even if ET0 is set, no interrupt is serviced and the pin never toggles. **(—1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Using wrong TMOD value for Timer 1** — TMOD = 0x10 for Timer 1 Mode 1 (NOT 0x01, which is Timer 0). **(—1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Forgetting `RETI`** — Using `RET` instead of `RETI` at the end of the ISR. `RET` does not clear the interrupt priority flag, leading to erroneous re-entry. **(—2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Square wave generation = periodic pin toggling driven by timer overflow interrupts.
- **Key Formula Chain:** $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc} \rightarrow N = T_{delay}/T_{mc} \rightarrow \text{Reload} = 65536 - N$.
- **Crystal Standard:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz} \Rightarrow T_{mc} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$.
- **Frequency Formula:** $f = 1 / (2 \times N \times T_{mc})$.
- **THx and TLx split:** $\text{THx} = (65536-N)/256$, $\text{TLx} = (65536-N) \mod 256$.
- **Critical SFRs:** `TMOD` (mode select), `TCON` (TRx to start, TFx overflow flag), `IE` (EA + ETx), `THx/TLx` (timer registers).
- **Mode 1 (16-bit):** Maximum delay = 65536 × $T_{mc}$ ≈ 71 ms. **Manual reload required in ISR.**
- **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload):** Maximum delay = 256 × $T_{mc}$ ≈ 278 µs. **No manual reload needed.**
- **ISR Vectors:** Timer 0 → 000BH, Timer 1 → 001BH.
- **Pin Toggle Instruction:** `CPL P1.x` (assembly) or `P1_x = ~P1_x;` (C).
- **Duty Cycle:** 50% requires symmetric timer reload; asymmetric duty cycle requires **alternating reload values** in the ISR (use a flag bit to track phase).
- **Always use `RETI` (not `RET`)** at the end of any ISR.
- **Most common KTU pin:** P1.0 (but P1.5, P1.2, P3.x are also frequently used).
- **Default high state:** Set the output pin HIGH before starting the timer to ensure the waveform begins on a rising edge.
- **Verification on CRO:** Use Channel 1 of the oscilloscope, set to 1V/div and appropriate timebase (e.g., 500 µs/div for 1 kHz).
